objective: Test whether providing written formative feedback three times per week instead of once per week improves end-of-term exam scores in an introductory university statistics course.
hard_constraints: quasi-experimental design comparing two sections of the same course taught by the same instructor

final_proposal:
# Final Research Proposal

---

## Core Hypothesis

When written corrective feedback is returned to students within 24 hours of a submission, the gap before their next related submission is short enough that the feedback remains functionally accessible — retrievable and applicable — at the moment of the next attempt. When that gap is approximately 96 hours, intervening lecture content and new problem sets displace the feedback before it can be applied. The accumulation of this difference in error-specific correction across a 13-week term produces a measurable advantage on a final exam testing the same reasoning skills.

**Formally:** Distributing a fixed quantity of written instructor feedback across three submissions per week, rather than returning the same quantity once per week, reduces the feedback-application lag sufficiently to increase error-specific correction rates, and this increase mediates a positive effect on final exam scores.

**The single causal chain:**

```
Condition → Feedback-Application Lag → Error-Specific Correction Rate → Final Exam Score
```

Each arrow is measured. No arrow is assumed.

**Directional predictions:**

1. Students in the 3×/week condition will have shorter experienced lags than students in the 1×/week condition (manipulation check)
2. Shorter experienced lag will predict higher error-specific correction rates across both conditions combined (mechanism test)
3. Error-specific correction rate will partially mediate the condition effect on final exam scores (outcome test)
4. The 3×/week condition will outperform the 1×/week condition on the final exam after controlling for baseline ability (primary estimand)

A null result on prediction 4 with a passed manipulation check is pre-specified as interpretable and publishable: it would indicate that feedback-application lag, even when successfully reduced, does not translate to exam-score gains at this dosage and timescale — a practically important finding for instructor workload policy.

---

## Minimal Viable Design

### Setting and Participants

One introductory university statistics course, one instructor, two concurrent sections in the same semester. Students self-select into sections by schedule availability. Condition is assigned to section by a pre-registered coin flip before the semester begins. Target enrollment: 70 students per section. Minimum retained sample: 50 per section (hard constraint). Term length: 13 weeks.

### Condition Assignment

| | Control (1×/week) | Treatment (3×/week) |
|---|---|---|
| Problem set structure | Nine problems in three thematic clusters of three, submitted together | Same nine problems in the same three clusters, submitted separately |
| Submission schedule | All nine problems, Friday 5 pm | Cluster A: Monday 5 pm; Cluster B: Wednesday 5 pm; Cluster C: Friday 5 pm |
| Feedback return | Written comments on all nine problems returned Monday 9 am | Written comments on each cluster returned within 24 hours of its deadline |
| Comments per problem | One substantive written comment with error-type code | One substantive written comment with error-type code |
| Total comments per student per week | 9 | 9 |
| Imposed feedback-application lag | ~96 hours (Monday feedback to Friday submission) | ~24 hours (feedback returned within 24 hours; next cluster due within 48 hours) |

Total feedback volume is identical across conditions. The manipulation is the temporal distribution of that volume, not its quantity. Problem set content, difficulty, lecture delivery, and all other instructional elements are identical across sections.

### Measures (Four Only)

**1. Experienced Lag (EL)**
Derived from LMS timestamps. T2 = first access of feedback document. T3 = first edit or view of next submission document in the same error-type category. EL = T3 − T2 in hours, aggregated as median per student across the term. Students who never access feedback are assigned EL = 168 hours and flagged; sensitivity analysis re-runs excluding them.

**2. Feedback Revision Index — Flagged (FRI-F)**
Proportion of feedback episodes in which the flagged error type is absent in the student's next submission on a problem of the same type. Computed only for episodes where a corrective comment (error codes E1–E6) was given and a subsequent same-type problem exists within the term. Two raters code a 25% random sample; target κ > 0.80.

**3. Baseline Covariates**
Pre-test score (20-item statistics readiness quiz, Week 0, identical across sections) and prior GPA (registrar records). Used as covariates in the primary ANCOVA and mechanism regression; used to construct propensity scores in the sensitivity analysis.

**4. Final Exam Score**
Total score 0–100 on an identical paper administered simultaneously in separate rooms in Week 13, graded by a single blinded marker using a pre-specified rubric. Ten percent of scripts double-marked; target κ > 0.80.

### Error-Type Coding Scheme

The instructor appends one of six pre-specified codes to each written comment at the time of writing. Codes are defined before the semester begins and locked in the pre-registration. They map onto the six major topic areas of the course.

| Code | Error Type | Course Topic |
|---|---|---|
| E1 | Distributional assumption error | Probability distributions |
| E2 | Interpretation error | Inference |
| E3 | Notation or formula error | Descriptive statistics |
| E4 | Procedural error | Hypothesis testing |
| E5 | Conceptual reasoning error | Regression |
| E6 | Data handling error | Data management |

Confirmatory comments (no error present) receive code C0 and are excluded from FRI-F computation. Coding burden: one character appended per comment, estimated under two seconds per feedback episode.

### Four Pre-Registered Analyses

**Analysis 1 — Manipulation check (Link 1):**
Independent samples t-test on median EL between conditions. Reported with Cohen's d and 95% CI. If d < 0.50 or p > .05, the manipulation is declared weak and all subsequent mechanism analyses are interpreted descriptively only, without causal language. This decision rule is locked in the pre-registration.

**Analysis 2 — Primary outcome:**
```
FinalExam_i = β0 + β1·Condition_i + β2·PreTest_i + β3·PriorGPA_i + ε_i
```
β1 is the primary estimand. Reported with Cohen's d (ANCOVA-adjusted) and 95% bootstrap CI (1,000 resamples). Two-tailed α = .05.

**Analysis 3 — Mechanism, lag predicts correction (Link 2):**
```
FRI-F_i = β0 + β1·EL_i + β2·PreTest_i + β3·PriorGPA_i + ε_i
```
Both conditions combined. Expected direction: negative. Reported with standardised β and 95% CI.

**Analysis 4 — Mechanism, mediation (Links 2 and 3 jointly):**
Simple mediation: Condition → FRI-F → FinalExam, with PreTest and PriorGPA as covariates on all paths. Bootstrapped indirect effect, 5,000 resamples. Reported: indirect effect, direct effect, proportion mediated, 95% bootstrap CI for indirect effect.

**Four pre-registered sensitivity analyses** (reported in supplement): IPTW propensity score adjustment; multiple imputation for attrition; exclusion of students below 70% submission rate; replacement of median EL with mean EL in Analysis 3.

No other analyses are conducted. Unplanned analyses are prohibited by pre-registration unless explicitly labelled as unplanned in a separate supplementary section.

### Protocol Timeline

```
Week 0
  Coin-flip condition assignment (witnessed, OSF-logged)
  Pre-registration locked (all analyses, decision rules, missing
    data handling, sensitivity analyses, κ targets)
  Instructor calibration: error-type coding on 30-comment
    practice set; κ must reach 0.85 before Week 1 begins
  Pre-test administered (both sections, identical instrument,
    same sitting window)
  Registrar provides prior GPA for all enrolled students

Weeks 1–13
  Identical lectures, identical problem sets
  Feedback delivered per condition schedule
  LMS logs T1, T2, T3 timestamps passively throughout
  Instructor appends error-type code to each comment
  RA double-codes 25% random sample of FRI-F episodes
    on a rolling basis (not end-loaded)

Week 6
  Internal data safety review: check for procedural failures
    (e.g., systematic feedback delays, section crossover)
  No interim analysis of outcome data
  No condition unblinding to students or grader

Week 13
  Final exam: identical paper, simultaneous administration,
    separate rooms, section identifiers stripped before grading
  Single blinded marker grades all scripts
  10% random sample double-marked for inter-rater reliability
```

### Power

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Target effect size | d = 0.40 |
| Pre-test to exam correlation | r = 0.50 (conservative) |
| ANCOVA variance reduction factor | 1 − r² = 0.75 |
| Required N per group (α = .05, power = .80) | 38 |
| Target enrollment per section | 70 |
| Achieved power at target N after 15% attrition | > 0.90 |
| Minimum retained N (hard constraint) | 50 per section; power = 0.70 at d = 0.40 |

---

## Primary Outcome

**Final exam score**, analysed with the pre-registered ANCOVA (Analysis 2). The primary estimand is β1, the condition coefficient after controlling for pre-test score and prior GPA. Reported as Cohen's d with 95% bootstrap confidence interval.

The final exam is the only outcome that reflects transfer of corrected understanding to novel problems under conditions where students cannot access prior feedback. Weekly problem set scores are not a primary or secondary outcome; they are the raw material from which FRI-F is computed. This distinction is important: FRI-F measures whether students correct errors when they have access to feedback; the final exam measures whether those corrections have been consolidated into durable knowledge.

---

## Main Confound and Mitigation

**The main confound is section-level self-selection.** Students choose which section to enroll in based on schedule availability, and schedule preference may correlate with characteristics that independently predict exam performance — employment hours, commuting time, course load, or chronotype. Because condition is assigned at the section level, any pre-existing difference between sections is a potential alternative explanation for a condition effect.

**Mitigation has three layers, applied in order of strength:**

**Layer 1 — Design:** Condition is assigned to section by coin flip after enrollment closes, not before. This means students cannot select into a condition; they select into a time slot, and the time slot is then randomly assigned to a condition. The randomisation does not eliminate pre-existing differences between sections, but it ensures those differences are not systematically related to condition.

**Layer 2 — Primary analysis:** The ANCOVA controls for pre-test score and prior GPA, the two individual-level variables most likely to mediate any selection effect on exam performance. If the sections differ on baseline ability, the covariate adjustment absorbs that difference before the condition coefficient is estimated.

**Layer 3 — Sensitivity analysis:** IPTW with a propensity score estimated from prior GPA, year of study, and STEM versus non-STEM major reweights the sample to approximate what would be observed if section assignment had been individual-level random. Agreement between the ANCOVA and IPTW estimates strengthens the causal interpretation; divergence is reported transparently as evidence of residual confounding.

No other confound receives this three-layer treatment because no other confound threatens the primary estimand with comparable plausibility. Engagement and instructor-attention confounds are ruled out structurally by holding all instructional elements constant across conditions; they are not given separate analytic layers, which would imply they are treated as equally serious threats.

---

## Two Plausible Alternatives Not Chosen

### Alternative 1: Consecutive-Semester Design (Prior Semester as Control)

**What it is:** The control condition is run in semester one with the instructor's existing 1×/week feedback practice. The treatment condition is run in semester two with 3×/week feedback. The same instructor teaches both semesters.

**Why it was seriously considered:** It eliminates the section self-selection confound entirely, because there is only one section per semester. It also allows the instructor to refine the error-type coding scheme between semesters, improving measurement quality.

**Why it was rejected:** Cohort non-equivalence is a more serious threat than section self-selection and cannot be mitigated analytically. Students in different semesters differ systematically in ways that are not captured by prior GPA or pre-test scores: the prior semester's students may have been affected by an external event (a public health disruption, a change in prerequisite course content, a shift in the student population's preparation level) that has nothing to do with feedback frequency. The ANCOVA and IPTW adjustments used in the concurrent design address individual-level confounds; they cannot address cohort-level confounds. The concurrent design's self-selection problem is the lesser threat.

### Alternative 2: Adding a Third Section Receiving Increased Feedback Volume (Same Frequency as Control)

**What it is:** A third section receives feedback once per week, as in the control condition, but the instructor writes three times as many comments per problem set — matching the total number of feedback interactions in the treatment condition but delivering them all at once.

**Why it was seriously considered:** It would allow the design to separate frequency from volume explicitly. If the third section (high-volume, low-frequency) performs no better than the control but worse than the treatment, the lag account is supported over a volume account. This is a cleaner isolation of the active ingredient than any analytic adjustment.

**Why it was rejected:** This alternative is explicitly banned by the hard constraints (no third section receiving a different feedback condition). Beyond the constraint, it would also require the instructor to write three times as many comments per problem in the third section, making the feedback qualitatively different in density and potentially in character — violating the spirit of the constraint against varying feedback content between sections. The design as proposed addresses the volume confound by holding total comment count constant across conditions, which is sufficient to rule out volume as an explanation without requiring a third section.

---

### Research Proposal: The Prediction-Feedback Discrepancy (PFD) Study

#### Core Hypothesis
The temporal delay in AI feedback improves the quality of human research-question generation only when it is preceded by an explicit prediction task. We hypothesize that the delay acts as an incubation window that maintains the participant’s cognitive engagement with their own predicted critique, thereby maximizing the "Prediction Error" signal—the discrepancy between the user's mental model and the AI’s actual feedback—which in turn drives more effective iterative refinement.

#### Minimal Viable Design
*   **Participants:** 100 participants (N=50/group).
*   **Task:** Participants read a complex scientific abstract and draft a research question. They then perform two mandatory stages:
    *   **Condition A (Control):** 10s "Self-Review" of their own draft $\rightarrow$ 10s AI Feedback (Immediate).
    *   **Condition B (Experimental):** 10s "Self-Review" of their own draft $\rightarrow$ 10s "Prediction" (User types what they expect the AI to critique) $\rightarrow$ 10s "Processing" (System delay).
*   **Standardization:** Both conditions are duration-matched (20s total). Both groups receive identical AI feedback. The interface uses a neutral, consistent "system activity" bar to ensure delay perception is uniform.

#### Primary Outcome
**Delta-Score:** The improvement in rubric-based quality (Novelty, Feasibility, Alignment) from the initial draft to the final refined draft, calculated by two blinded, independent reviewers.

#### Main Confound and Mitigation
*   **Confound:** **"Prediction Effect."** The quality improvement in Condition B might be attributed to the act of writing a prediction, not the 10-second delay.
*   **Mitigation:** The control group (A) is required to perform a "Self-Review" task of equal duration and effort, ensuring that the *time-on-task* is identical. By comparing the delta-scores between these two configurations, the specific causal contribution of the temporal "incubation" gap is isolated.

#### Two Plausible Alternatives Not Chosen
1.  **Dose-Response Variation (Short vs. Long Delay):** We rejected this because it fails to test the *mechanism* of cognitive engagement, focusing only on the *timing*, which would leave reviewers questioning why the delay works (or if it simply increases frustration).
2.  **No-AI Control (Human-only vs. AI-assisted):** We rejected this as it does not isolate the effect of *delayed AI* responses. The research brief specifically asks to test the delay effect; comparing against a non-AI control would conflate the effect of the AI itself with the effect of the delay.

final_proposal:
**Final Proposal**

**Core Hypothesis**
The core hypothesis of this study is that structured intergroup contact with outgroup members, particularly when status-equal, reduces implicit racial bias by increasing empathy and perspective-taking towards the outgroup.

**Minimal Viable Design**
The minimal viable design for this study is a randomized controlled trial with two arms: (1) a cooperative task with a status-equal outgroup partner, and (2) a solo task control. The intervention will be a single session lasting no more than 90 minutes, and the follow-up measurement will be at exactly 2 weeks post-intervention.

**Primary Outcome**
The primary outcome of this study is the reduction in implicit racial bias, as measured by both the Implicit Association Test (IAT) and a behavioral measure of implicit bias (Shooter Bias Task).

**Main Confound and Mitigation**
The main confound in this study is the potential for participant self-selection bias, where participants who are more motivated to reduce their implicit bias may be more likely to choose the intervention arm. To mitigate this, we will use a randomized controlled trial design, where participants are randomly assigned to either the intervention or control arm. Additionally, we will use a manipulation check to ensure that the intervention is successfully manipulating the variables of interest.

**Two Plausible Alternatives Not Chosen**
Two plausible alternatives that were not chosen for this study are:

1. Structured perspective-taking conversation with an outgroup partner vs. unstructured free conversation control: This design was not chosen because it may not be as effective in promoting mutual interdependence and shared goals as the cooperative task.
2. Joint problem-solving exercise with status-equal outgroup partner vs. passive exposure documentary-viewing control: This design was not chosen because it may not be as engaging and interactive as the cooperative task, and may not promote the same level of empathy and perspective-taking.

## Core hypothesis
In healthy adults aged 18–35, **36 hours of continuous wakefulness** will **causally reduce working-memory updating accuracy** on the N-back task, with a **larger impairment at 3-back than 2-back** relative to a rested control night.

---

## Minimal viable design
### Study type
**Randomized between-subjects design**

### Groups
- **Sleep-deprived group:** 36 hours of continuous wakefulness
- **Rested control group:** 7–9 hours sleep before testing

### Sample
- **25 participants per group**  
  This exceeds the minimum requirement of 20 per group and leaves modest room for exclusions while staying feasible.

### Participants
- Healthy adults aged **18–35**
- No diagnosed sleep disorders

### Sleep verification
- **Wrist actigraphy** for both groups
- Deprivation group must show **36 hours continuous wakefulness**
- Control group must show **7–9 hours sleep** the night before testing

### Testing
- Single session between **08:00 and 10:00**
- Standard N-back task with:
  - **2-back**
  - **3-back**
- **Task order counterbalanced** across participants

### Compliance rules
- No **caffeine, alcohol, or stimulants** in the 24 hours before testing
- Participants failing actigraphy requirements are excluded from the primary analysis

### Analysis
- Primary mechanistic test: **group × load interaction** on d-prime
- Prespecified between-group comparisons at:
  - 2-back
  - 3-back

---

## Primary outcome
**d-prime** on:
- **2-back**
- **3-back**

Reaction time is **secondary only**.

The key confirmatory target is whether the sleep-deprived group shows a lower d-prime than controls, especially on **3-back**, with the expectation that the **3-back effect size exceeds 0.5**.

---

## Main confound and mitigation
### Main confound
**Pre-existing individual differences in baseline working memory ability** could make group differences look like a deprivation effect when they are partly due to starting differences.

### Mitigation
- **Randomize** participants to group
- Use a **single standardized testing window** between 08:00 and 10:00
- Use **actigraphy-verified compliance**
- **Counterbalance N-back order**
- If feasible within the same study workflow, collect a brief **baseline N-back measure** at screening and use it as a covariate in sensitivity analysis

If you want the strict minimal version, the combination of randomization, actigraphy, and counterbalancing is the essential mitigation. Baseline measurement is the stronger optional safeguard.

---

## Two plausible alternatives not chosen
### 1) Within-subject crossover design
**Why it was not chosen:**  
It would improve power, but it increases complexity, introduces washout/carryover concerns, and makes the causal story less clean for a first-pass study.

### 2) Add a 24-hour partial deprivation arm
**Why it was not chosen:**  
It would be useful for dose-response inference, but it adds a new comparison and complicates the study beyond the minimal design needed to answer the main question.

---

**Final Proposal**

**Core Hypothesis**:
Dual-task interference on a primary visual working memory task is larger when the concurrent secondary task is visual rather than auditory, due to shared reliance on visual working memory resources.

**Minimal Viable Design**:
A within-subjects design with exactly 5 conditions: a single-task baseline (primary task only), a visual dual-task condition, an auditory dual-task condition, a primary-task priority condition, and a secondary-task priority condition. Each participant will complete all conditions in a counterbalanced order to control for order effects.

**Primary Outcome**:
Change-detection sensitivity (d-prime) compared across the five conditions, with a focus on how emotional content, trial type, and task priority influence performance under different dual-task conditions.

**Main Confound and Mitigation**:
A potential confound is the difficulty of the secondary tasks, which could affect the magnitude of dual-task interference. To mitigate this, the secondary tasks will be matched in difficulty through pilot calibration, and the task-priority manipulation will be used to examine the causal direction of the observed effects.

**Two Plausible Alternatives Not Chosen**:
1. **Individual-differences analysis**: Correlating working memory capacity with interference magnitude, which was not chosen due to the constraints of the study and the focus on the core hypothesis.
2. **Cross-modal secondary task condition**: Adding a condition where the secondary task is both visual and auditory, which was not chosen due to the constraints of the study and the potential for increased complexity and confounds.

### Final Research Proposal: Fragmentation Effects on Bee Species Richness

**Core Hypothesis**
Increasing habitat fragmentation, operationalized as decreasing mean patch area of semi-natural habitat within a 1 km radius, leads to a significant decline in wild bee species richness in temperate agricultural landscapes, independent of landscape-level crop diversity.

**Minimal Viable Design**
*   **Sampling:** 40 agricultural sites within a 200 km diameter region, stratified into quartiles of mean patch area to ensure gradient coverage.
*   **Protocol:** Standardized pan-trap arrays (UV-yellow, blue, white) deployed for 24-hour periods during three phenological blocks.
*   **Metrics:** Species-level identification of all captured bees.
*   **Analysis:** Coverage-based rarefaction (Hill numbers $q=0$) to standardize richness, followed by a Poisson or Negative Binomial Generalized Linear Model (GLM) to regress rarefied richness against mean patch area, reporting $\beta$ coefficients and 95% confidence intervals.

**Primary Outcome**
A quantifiable effect size (slope coefficient) indicating the relationship between mean patch size and bee species richness, accompanied by 95% confidence intervals and a visualization of the regression with partial residuals.

**Main Confound and Mitigation**
*   **Confound:** Landscape-level crop diversity (Shannon index of crop types within 1 km), which may independently drive bee richness by providing varied floral resources.
*   **Mitigation:** Inclusion of crop diversity as an additive covariate in the GLM. Likelihood Ratio Tests will be used to assess the improvement in model fit, and partial regression plots will isolate the effect of patch area from the influence of crop heterogeneity.

**Two Plausible Alternatives Not Chosen**
1.  **Structural Equation Modeling (SEM):** While useful for mapping direct and indirect paths (e.g., fragmentation $\to$ isolation $\to$ richness), it requires complex path specification and larger sample sizes than the 40-site minimum to ensure model stability and avoid overfitting.
2.  **Hierarchical Distance Sampling:** While superior for estimating detection probability, it requires distance measurements for each bee encounter. This necessitates high-density sampling or longer observation times that exceed the "minimal sufficient complexity" needed to test the primary fragmentation hypothesis.

# Research Proposal: Sim-to-Real Transfer via Domain Randomization vs. Real-World Fine-Tuning for Robotic Grasping

---

## Core Hypothesis

A simulation-pretrained grasping policy deployed zero-shot with domain randomization (DR) will achieve a **higher grasp success rate** on novel household objects than the same base policy fine-tuned on ≤500 real-world grasp attempts, because domain randomization exposes the policy to a broader distribution of physical and visual variation than a small real-world dataset can provide—preventing overfitting to the narrow real-world training distribution while maintaining generalization to unseen objects.

---

## Minimal Viable Design

### Hardware Platform
**Franka Panda (7-DOF arm, parallel-jaw gripper)** deployed in a single fixed workspace with an overhead RGB-D camera (Intel RealSense D435). This platform has well-maintained Isaac Gym support and a large community codebase, minimizing infrastructure risk.

### Base Policy Architecture (Shared by Both Conditions)
- **Architecture:** A convolutional encoder (ResNet-18) processing RGB-D input → MLP policy head outputting 6-DOF grasp pose (position + quaternion) + gripper open/close action.
- **Simulation pretraining:** Both conditions begin from **identical weights** produced by behavioral cloning on scripted expert grasps in Isaac Gym (same simulation, same random seed initialization).
- The base policy is trained in simulation until it achieves ≥80% success on held-out simulation objects before branching into the two experimental conditions. This checkpoint is frozen as the shared starting point.

---

### Condition A — Domain Randomization (Zero-Shot Transfer)

**Simulator:** Isaac Gym (GPU-accelerated, PhysX backend).

**Randomization parameters (minimum required; sampled uniformly at each episode):**

| Parameter | Range |
|---|---|
| Texture | 200+ procedurally generated textures applied to objects, table, and background |
| Lighting | 1–4 point lights; intensity 0.3–2.0 normalized units; color temperature 3000–7000 K; random azimuth/elevation |
| Object mass | ±50% of nominal mesh-estimated mass |
| Friction (object–gripper, object–table) | μ ∈ [0.2, 1.5] |
| Camera pose | ±5° tilt, ±3 cm translation from nominal |
| Object pose at placement | Full SE(2) randomization on table surface |

The policy is **not updated after simulation training**. It is deployed directly to the physical Franka Panda with no real-world gradient steps.

---

### Condition B — Real-World Fine-Tuning

**Starting point:** Identical simulation-pretrained weights as Condition A.

**Fine-tuning method:** Behavioral cloning (BC) on real-world demonstrations collected via kinesthetic teaching (human physically guides the arm). BC is chosen over DAgger to avoid requiring an interactive expert during deployment, keeping the method self-contained and the budget tractable.

**Budget:** Exactly **500 grasp attempts** collected across a **training object set of 20 household objects** (distinct from the 30 test objects). Demonstrations are split 90/10 train/validation; fine-tuning stops at the epoch minimizing validation loss or at 200 epochs, whichever comes first. No additional real-world data is collected after this budget is exhausted.

**Why BC over DAgger:** DAgger requires an interactive expert at deployment time, which introduces an uncontrolled variable (expert consistency). BC on a fixed 500-attempt dataset provides a cleaner budget boundary and is directly comparable to the zero-shot condition.

---

### Test Set Construction

- **30 household objects**, none present in simulation training scenes, domain randomization object pools, or real-world fine-tuning training objects.
- Objects are sourced from the **YCB object set** supplemented by common kitchen/office items purchased locally to ensure novelty.
- Objects are categorized into three difficulty tiers by two independent raters blind to experimental condition, using physical dimensions and shape regularity:

| Category | Criterion | N objects |
|---|---|---|
| Small | Longest dimension < 7 cm (e.g., bolt, eraser, bottle cap) | 10 |
| Medium | Longest dimension 7–15 cm (e.g., mug, stapler, banana) | 10 |
| Large | Longest dimension > 15 cm (e.g., cereal box, water bottle, pan) | 10 |

- **Inter-rater reliability** (Cohen's κ) reported; disagreements resolved by a third rater.

---

### Evaluation Protocol

- **Attempts per object per condition:** 10 grasp attempts minimum (→ 300 attempts per condition total).
- **Attempt procedure:** Object placed in a standardized pose by the experimenter at the start of each attempt (randomized from 5 pre-defined stable poses per object, assigned in counterbalanced order).
- **Success criterion:** Gripper lifts object ≥10 cm above table surface **and** holds for ≥3 seconds without dropping, as measured by the arm's end-effector height encoder and a binary drop sensor (force/torque threshold on the wrist).
- **Condition order:** Counterbalanced across objects (half evaluated A-first, half B-first) to control for object wear and environmental drift.
- **Experimenter blinding:** The person recording success/failure is unaware of which condition produced the grasp (camera feed only; robot identity obscured by identical workspace setup).

---

### Statistical Analysis

- **Primary comparison:** Two-proportion z-test comparing overall grasp success rates (Condition A vs. B), with **95% confidence intervals** computed via Wilson score interval for each condition.
- **Per-category analysis:** Success rates and 95% CIs reported separately for small, medium, and large categories; a 2×3 contingency table (condition × difficulty) analyzed with a chi-square test.
- **Object-level random effects:** Mixed-effects logistic regression with object as a random intercept, condition and difficulty as fixed effects, to account for non-independence of repeated attempts on the same object.
- **Effect size:** Cohen's h for the primary proportion comparison.
- **Significance threshold:** α = 0.05, two-tailed; no correction for the primary comparison (pre-registered as single primary outcome); Bonferroni correction applied to the three within-category comparisons.

---

## Primary Outcome

**Per-method grasp success rate (proportion of successful lifts) across all 30 test objects, with 95% Wilson confidence intervals**, broken down by object difficulty category (small/medium/large, ≥8 objects each → 10 objects each in this design). The primary inferential question is whether the confidence intervals of the two conditions overlap and whether the z-test reaches significance at α = 0.05.

---

## Main Confound and Mitigation

### Confound: Unequal Exposure to Object Geometry Diversity

**Mechanism:** The fine-tuning condition (Condition B) is trained on 20 specific real-world objects, potentially including objects whose geometry is incidentally similar to some test objects, giving it an unfair advantage on those test items. Conversely, domain randomization operates over a fixed mesh library in simulation that may over- or under-represent certain shape classes, biasing its generalization in a different direction.

**Mitigation (three-part):**

1. **Geometric distance screening:** Before finalizing the training and test splits, compute the convex hull volume, aspect ratio, and surface curvature histogram of all candidate objects. Use a greedy maximum-diversity algorithm to ensure the 20 fine-tuning training objects and 30 test objects are maximally dissimilar in this feature space (minimum pairwise cosine distance threshold = 0.3). The simulation mesh library is similarly screened to exclude meshes within this threshold of any test object.

2. **Stratified reporting:** Report success rates conditioned on geometric similarity (nearest-neighbor distance in feature space) between each test object and each method's training distribution. This allows post-hoc assessment of whether any advantage is explained by accidental proximity.

3. **Pre-registration:** The full object assignment (training vs. test, category labels, geometric distances) is locked and time-stamped before any real-world data collection begins, preventing post-hoc reassignment.

---

## Two Plausible Alternatives Not Chosen

### Alternative 1: NVIDIA Omniverse + Photorealistic Rendering on UR5e with Residual Policy

**Description:** Use Omniverse Replicator for physically-based rendering domain randomization (ray-traced textures, HDR lighting) and deploy to a UR5e arm via a learned residual policy that corrects for sim-to-real dynamics gaps at runtime.

**Reason not chosen:** The residual policy layer introduces a partial real-world adaptation component that blurs the conceptual boundary between the two experimental conditions—the residual policy itself requires real-world interaction data to train, making it a hybrid approach that edges toward a banned move (combining DR and real-world fine-tuning). Additionally, the UR5e has weaker Isaac Gym integration than the Franka Panda, increasing infrastructure complexity without improving internal validity.

---

### Alternative 2: Dynamics Randomization Only in MuJoCo + DAgger Fine-Tuning

**Description:** Implement domain randomization restricted to dynamics parameters only (mass, friction, joint damping) in MuJoCo—no visual randomization—and compare against fine-tuning with DAgger on real robot data.

**Reason not chosen:** This design violates the hard constraint that domain randomization must vary **at least** texture, lighting, object mass, and friction. Omitting visual randomization (texture, lighting) means the DR condition does not satisfy the minimum randomization requirement. DAgger is also excluded from the fine-tuning condition because it requires an interactive expert at deployment time, introducing experimenter-consistency confounds and making the 500-attempt budget harder to define cleanly (query thresholds, switching criteria).

---

objective: Test whether providing written formative feedback three times per week instead of once per week improves end-of-term exam scores in an introductory university statistics course.
hard_constraints: quasi-experimental design comparing two sections of the same course taught by the same instructor
minimum 50 students per section
feedback must be written comments on weekly problem sets, not grades or scores alone
final exam must be identical across both sections and graded by a blinded marker
study duration is exactly one academic term (10 to 15 weeks)
no additional tutoring or supplementary instruction offered to either group
banned_moves: add a third section receiving peer feedback instead of instructor feedback
measure student satisfaction or perceived learning as a co-primary outcome
vary the content or difficulty of the problem sets between sections
use automated AI-generated feedback instead of instructor-written feedback
final_proposal:
Here is the final research proposal.

### Core Hypothesis
Providing written formative feedback three times per week improves end-of-term exam scores in an introductory university statistics course compared to providing feedback once per week, assuming practice spacing, feedback volume, and baseline ability are held constant.

### Minimal Viable Design
A quasi-experimental design comparing two concurrent sections (N $\ge$ 50 each) of an introductory statistics course taught by the same instructor over a single 10-to-15-week term. Entire sections are randomly assigned to either the Treatment or Control condition.

**Intervention Framework:**
Both sections submit identical weekly problem sets in three mandatory chunks (Monday, Wednesday, Friday).
*   **Treatment (High-Frequency):** The instructor provides written formative comments within 24 hours of each submission (three times per week).
*   **Control (Standard):** The instructor reviews all three submissions but provides a single, consolidated document of written formative comments once per week (e.g., Saturday).

**Fidelity & Process Tracking:** 
*   **Uptake:** LMS access logs will calculate the proportion of students who open their feedback within 48 hours of posting.
*   **Volume:** Total weekly instructor feedback word count per student will be extracted to verify the instructor provided equivalent total feedback across both conditions. 
*   *Strict Limitations:* No scores/grades on these problem sets, no automated AI feedback, and no supplementary instruction/tutoring for either group.

### Primary Outcome
The primary outcome is the score on an identical end-of-term final exam, evaluated by an independent marker blinded to student identity and section. 

Following a pre-registered analysis plan, the scores will be evaluated using an ANCOVA to control for baseline statistical ability (measured in Week 1 via the validated CAOS pre-test):
`Final_Exam_Score ~ Condition + CAOS_Pre_Test_Score`
The main effect of the intervention will be extracted, converted to Cohen’s *d*, and reported alongside its 95% Confidence Interval.

### Main Confound and Mitigation
**The Confound: Spaced Practice vs. Spaced Feedback**
In a standard classroom environment, high-frequency feedback typically requires high-frequency student submissions. If the Treatment group submitted work three times a week and the Control group submitted work once a week, any observed exam improvement could be attributed to the well-documented "spacing effect" of distributed practice rather than the frequency of the feedback.

**The Mitigation: Holding the Submission Schedule Constant**
To rigorously isolate the causal mechanism, the design mandates that *both* groups adhere to the exact same Monday/Wednesday/Friday submission schedule. By forcing the Control group to practice at the exact same frequency as the Treatment group, the *only* independent variable is the delivery schedule of the instructor's feedback.

### Two Plausible Alternatives Not Chosen
1.  **Propensity Score Matching (PSM) using prior GPA:** We considered using PSM to adjust for selection bias between the two sections. This was rejected because PSM discards unmatched students, which risks violating the hard constraint of maintaining N $\ge$ 50 students per section. Furthermore, using a domain-specific baseline covariate (the CAOS pre-test) in an ANCOVA provides superior precision and statistical power without sample attrition.
2.  **Historical control using a consecutive semester:** We considered comparing a current semester's section to the immediate prior semester's section to avoid cross-contamination. This was rejected because it introduces temporal confounds (history bias and cohort effects). Randomly assigning two concurrent sections entirely eliminates semester-specific variables (e.g., term dynamics, campus events) with minimal sufficient complexity.

***

objective: Test whether sim-to-real transfer with domain randomization achieves higher grasp success rate on novel household objects than fine-tuning a simulation-pretrained policy on 500 or fewer real-world grasps.
hard_constraints: evaluation on exactly 30 previously unseen household objects not present in any training or randomization set
both methods must start from the same base policy architecture and simulation-pretrained weights
real-world fine-tuning budget capped at 500 grasp attempts total
domain randomization must vary at least texture, lighting, object mass, and friction parameters
robot hardware must be a single 6-DOF or 7-DOF arm with a parallel-jaw gripper, no suction or dexterous hands
grasp success defined as lifting the object at least 10 cm above the table and holding for 3 seconds
each test object must be evaluated with at least 10 grasp attempts per method
banned_moves: add a third method or hybrid that combines domain randomization and real-world fine-tuning

expand the test set to include deformable or articulated objects
collect more than 500 real-world grasps for the fine-tuning condition
final_proposal:
## Core hypothesis
A simulation-pretrained grasping policy trained with **domain randomization** will achieve a higher **real-world grasp success rate** on **30 novel household objects** than the same base policy **fine-tuned on up to 500 real-world grasp attempts**.

## Minimal viable design
### Study type
A two-arm comparison with the **same base policy architecture** and the **same simulation-pretrained weights** at initialization for both methods.

### Methods compared
1. **Domain-randomized sim-to-real transfer**
   - Train in simulation only.
   - Use domain randomization over at least:
     - texture
     - lighting
     - object mass
     - friction
   - Deploy zero-shot to the real robot.

2. **Simulation-pretrained policy fine-tuned on real grasps**
   - Start from the same simulation-pretrained weights.
   - Fine-tune using **no more than 500 real-world grasp attempts total**.
   - No domain randomization requirement here beyond the shared simulation pretraining.

### Robot and hardware
- One **6-DOF or 7-DOF arm** with a **parallel-jaw gripper**
- No suction, no dexterous hand

### Candidate implementation choice
The simplest compliant option is:
- **Franka Panda arm** for real-world evaluation
- **Isaac Gym** for simulation training and domain randomization

This is preferable because it satisfies the hardware constraint directly and avoids adding extra complexity from residual policies or photorealistic rendering pipelines.

### Test set
- **Exactly 30 previously unseen household objects**
- None may appear in any training or randomization set
- No deformable or articulated objects
- To satisfy the difficulty breakdown requirement:
  - 10 small
  - 10 medium
  - 10 large

### Evaluation protocol
- For each of the 30 objects:
  - evaluate **at least 10 grasp attempts per method**
- Use the same grasp success definition for both methods:
  - object lifted at least **10 cm above the table**
  - held for **3 seconds**

### Data collection budget
- Fine-tuning condition only:
  - cap at **500 real-world grasp attempts total**
- Domain-randomized method:
  - no real-world fine-tuning

### Reporting
Report:
- per-method grasp success rate
- **95% confidence intervals**
- breakdown by object difficulty category:
  - small
  - medium
  - large

## Primary outcome
The primary outcome is the **real-world grasp success rate** on the 30 held-out household objects, compared between:
- domain-randomized zero-shot transfer
- simulation-pretrained policy fine-tuned on ≤500 real grasps

The main analysis should compare:
- pooled success rate across all test objects
- category-stratified success rates for small, medium, and large objects
- 95% confidence intervals for each method

## Main confound and mitigation
### Main confound
The biggest confound is **object-level difficulty imbalance** between methods and across categories, especially because grasp success can vary sharply with size, shape, surface texture, and pose stability.

### Mitigation
- Use the **same 30 objects** for both methods
- Keep the same number of attempts per object per method
- Stratify the test set into **small/medium/large** with at least 8 objects per category; a balanced 10/10/10 split is cleaner
- Randomize trial order across methods and objects
- Keep grasp pose sampling and placement protocol identical across methods
- Report object-level results in addition to pooled results to reduce misleading aggregation

## Two plausible alternatives not chosen
### Alternative 1: Photorealistic rendering-based domain randomization in Omniverse with a learned residual policy
- This is plausible and compliant in principle.
- Not chosen because it adds unnecessary complexity: the residual policy introduces another moving part that can blur attribution of any gain to domain randomization itself.
- The Isaac Gym zero-shot design is simpler and more directly tests the target claim.

### Alternative 2: Dynamics-only randomization in MuJoCo compared against DAgger fine-tuning
- Also plausible.
- Not chosen because it violates the stated requirement that domain randomization must vary at least **texture and lighting** as well as mass and friction.
- It would therefore fail a hard constraint and cannot answer the brief as written.

event study or difference-in-differences design only
sample must include at least 200 firms
estimation window must not overlap with the 2008 financial crisis
cost of equity must be estimated using an implied cost of capital model, not CAPM beta
analysis period must not exceed 10 years

final_proposal:
## Core hypothesis
**Mandatory ESG disclosure requirements reduce firms’ implied cost of equity capital by improving the public information environment and lowering forecast uncertainty.**

This is a narrow causal claim about disclosure mandates, not about ESG performance or voluntary ESG adoption.

---

## Minimal viable design
### Design
A **local difference-in-differences** design around the **EU Non-Financial Reporting Directive (NFRD) employee threshold**.

### Treatment group
EU listed **non-financial firms just above the NFRD threshold** that are legally required to disclose nonfinancial/ESG information.

### Control group
Comparable **non-financial firms just below the threshold** that are not subject to the mandate.

### Sample
- At least **200 firms**
- Exclude **financial-sector firms**
- Use only firms with publicly available stock price data and filings
- Restrict to a **narrow bandwidth around the threshold** to improve comparability

### Time window
A panel such as **2011–2020** or **2012–2021**, depending on data availability, so the analysis:
- does not overlap with the 2008 financial crisis,
- and stays within the 10-year cap.

### Protocol
1. Estimate the effect of mandate exposure on implied cost of equity.
2. Test dynamic pre-trends and post-treatment effects.
3. Run placebo thresholds and narrow-bandwidth robustness checks.
4. Test the mechanism using analyst forecast dispersion.

---

## Primary outcome
**Implied cost of equity capital**, estimated from stock prices and forecast data using a standard implied cost of capital model.

Recommended main specification:
- **Ohlson-Juettner-Nauroth (OJ)** model

Robustness can use another ICC model, but the paper should keep **one primary outcome variable**.

---

## Main confound and mitigation
### Main confound
**Concurrent regulatory changes** that may affect disclosure quality, investor information, or cost of equity around the same time as the NFRD.

### Mitigation
Use a combination of:
- **narrow bandwidth around the threshold**
- **firm fixed effects and year fixed effects**
- **event-time pre-trend tests**
- **placebo threshold tests**
- **sample restrictions or fixed effects for jurisdictions with overlapping reforms**
- explicit exclusion or sensitivity checks for countries with major concurrent reporting changes

This keeps the causal story focused on the mandate rather than broader regulatory shifts.

---

## Two plausible alternatives not chosen
### 1) Broad treated-vs-control DiD around the EU NFRD
**Why not chosen:**  
Feasible, but less credible than the threshold-based design because treated and control firms would differ more in size, reporting environment, and baseline disclosure behavior. Reviewers would be more likely to question selection and voluntary adoption.

### 2) Staggered DiD across multiple countries using Callaway-Sant’Anna
**Why not chosen:**  
Potentially powerful, but more complex and harder to keep clean under the hard constraints. It also raises more concerns about cross-country regulatory confounding and implementation heterogeneity, which weakens the minimal viable version.

---

objective: Determine whether long-term ambient PM2.5 exposure is associated with increased childhood asthma incidence after adjusting for socioeconomic and indoor-environment confounders.
hard_constraints: retrospective cohort design only, no randomization of exposure
exposure metric must be annual-average PM2.5 at residential address, not self-reported
cohort must include at least 5000 children aged 0-12
must use ICD-coded asthma diagnoses, not survey-reported symptoms
must adjust for at least household income and parental smoking status
no proprietary air-quality datasets; only publicly available EPA AQS or equivalent
banned_moves: add an indoor air-quality monitoring component to the study
expand the outcome to include all respiratory conditions rather than asthma specifically
incorporate genetic or biomarker data to explore biologic mechanisms
final_proposal:
**Final Research Proposal**

---

### **Core Hypothesis**

Long-term exposure to ambient PM2.5 is associated with an increased incidence of childhood asthma, independent of socioeconomic status and parental smoking, with each 5 µg/m³ increase in annual average PM2.5 concentration leading to a measurable rise in asthma hazard after adjusting for key confounders.

---

### **Minimal Viable Design**

A retrospective cohort study of 7,000 children aged 0–12 years, assembled from electronic health record (EHR) data across multiple U.S. pediatric healthcare systems (e.g., PEDSnet sites), with geocoded residential addresses and longitudinal follow-up. Annual average ambient PM2.5 exposure is assigned using inverse distance weighting of publicly available EPA Air Quality System (AQS) monitor data within 50 km of each child’s residence. Asthma incidence is defined by the first ICD-10 code for asthma (J45.x or J46.x) after cohort entry and preceded by at least 12 months without diagnosis. Children are followed from birth or study entry until asthma diagnosis, disenrollment, age 18, or end of data availability.

The primary analysis uses a **Cox proportional hazards model with time-varying PM2.5 exposure**, adjusted for household income (individual- or ZIP-code-level) and parental smoking status, along with child sex, race/ethnicity, birth year, and urbanicity. The model is pre-specified and locked prior to analysis.

---

### **Primary Outcome**

- **Definition**: Incident asthma diagnosis based on first recorded ICD-10 code J45.x (asthma) or J46.x (status asthmaticus) in the EHR, occurring after cohort entry and after a minimum 12-month observation period without prior asthma diagnosis.
- **Timing**: Time-to-event from cohort entry (birth or first visit) to earliest of: asthma diagnosis, loss to follow-up, age 18, or study end (e.g., 2023).
- **Analysis Metric**: Adjusted hazard ratio (HR) per 5 µg/m³ increase in annual average PM2.5, with 95% confidence interval.

---

### **Main Confound and Mitigation**

- **Main Confound**: Socioeconomic status (SES) is strongly associated with both PM2.5 exposure (e.g., urban, low-income neighborhoods often have higher pollution) and asthma risk (via access to care, stress, comorbidities). Unmeasured SES factors could bias the association.
  
- **Mitigation**:
  - **Direct adjustment** for household income (preferred) or ZIP-code-level median income from the American Community Survey (ACS).
  - **Additional adjustment** for Medicaid enrollment, parental education (if available), and neighborhood urbanicity.
  - **Sensitivity analysis** including neighborhood-level covariates (e.g., % college-educated, housing density) to assess residual confounding.
  - **Transparency**: Report E-value or discuss magnitude of unmeasured confounding needed to explain away the result.

---

### **Two Plausible Alternatives Not Chosen**

1. **Propensity Score–Matched Cohort Design (High vs. Low PM2.5 ZIP Codes)**  
   - *Why considered*: Reduces imbalance in observed confounders; intuitive for policy audiences.  
   - *Why rejected*: Inefficient (discards data), reduces power, and may introduce collider bias if matching induces spurious associations. Regression adjustment in a full cohort is more precise and better suited to time-varying exposure. Not necessary given large sample size and ability to adjust directly.

2. **Distributed Lag Nonlinear Model (DLNM) Using Satellite-Derived PM2.5**  
   - *Why considered*: Higher spatial resolution; captures nonlinear and delayed effects of PM2.5; widely used in environmental epidemiology.  
   - *Why rejected*: Relies on modeled or satellite-derived datasets (e.g., MAIAC, Downscaler) that, while publicly accessible, may not meet the "equivalent to EPA AQS" standard due to proprietary algorithms or lack of regulatory endorsement. Violates hard constraint on data sources. Also over-complex for a primary question about long-term annual average exposure.

---
