- **Data source:** U.S. EPA Air Quality System (AQS) publicly available monitoring data (https://www.epa.gov/aqs). Where monitor density is insufficient, the EPA's pre-generated annual summary files will be supplemented with the publicly available CDC/EPA Fused Air Quality Surface Using Downscaling (FAQSD) or the EPA's downscaler model outputs—both are non-proprietary federal products.
- **Spatial linkage:** Inverse-distance-weighted (IDW) interpolation from all AQS monitors within a 50-km radius of the geocoded residential centroid, using the three nearest monitors. This is the pre-specified primary exposure assignment rule.
- **Temporal window:** Annual averages for each calendar year the child is under observation, starting from birth year or 2000 (whichever is later), through age 12 or first asthma diagnosis (whichever is earlier).

### Outcome Ascertainment
- **Primary outcome:** Incident asthma diagnosis, defined as ≥ 2 ICD-coded encounters with a principal or secondary diagnosis of asthma (ICD-10: J45.20–J45.998; ICD-9: 493.0x–493.9x) within any rolling 12-month window, with no prior asthma code in the 24 months before cohort entry (wash-out period).
- The two-encounter rule is pre-specified to reduce miscoding; a single-encounter definition will be tested in sensitivity analysis.

### Primary Statistical Model
**Pre-specified primary model (registered before any outcome data are accessed):**

Cox proportional hazards model with time-varying PM2.5 exposure.

- **Time scale:** Age in days from cohort entry to first qualifying asthma diagnosis, administrative censoring (end of enrollment or age 13), or death.
- **Exposure term:** Annual-average PM2.5 as a continuous time-varying covariate, entered as a natural cubic spline (3 knots at the 10th, 50th, and 90th percentiles of the exposure distribution) to allow nonlinearity, with a pre-specified linear term reported as the primary effect estimate (HR per 5 µg/m³ increment).
- **Covariate adjustment (minimum required set):**
  | Confounder | Source | Operationalization |
  |---|---|---|
  | Household income | Claims/EHR enrollment data or census-tract median income (ACS 5-year) | Quintiles |
  | Parental smoking status | ICD codes for tobacco exposure (Z57.31, Z77.22) or documented smoking history in EHR | Binary (any vs. none) |
  | Sex | Administrative record | Binary |
  | Race/ethnicity | Administrative record | 5-category |
  | Urban/rural classification | USDA RUCC codes | 3-level |
  | Calendar year of birth | Administrative | Continuous |
  | Health system region | Enrollment file | Fixed effect |
- **Robust variance:** Sandwich estimator clustered on ZIP code of residence to account for spatial correlation of both exposure and unmeasured neighborhood factors.
- **Proportional hazards assumption:** Tested via Schoenfeld residuals; if violated for PM2.5, a time × exposure interaction term will be added (pre-specified contingency).

### Sensitivity Analyses (pre-specified, minimum required)
1. **Modified exposure window (required by success criteria):** Replace annual-average PM2.5 with a 3-year trailing moving average (years t, t−1, t−2) to capture cumulative lag effects; re-estimate the primary Cox model with this alternative exposure metric. Compare HR and CI to primary result.
2. **Outcome definition:** Repeat primary analysis using a single ICD-coded encounter as the asthma case definition.
3. **Exposure assignment:** Replace IDW interpolation with assignment from the single nearest AQS monitor (≤ 50 km) to test sensitivity to spatial smoothing.
4. **Unmeasured confounding:** E-value calculation for the primary HR to quantify the minimum confounding association needed to fully explain away the observed association.

---

## Primary Outcome

**Incident ICD-coded asthma diagnosis:** first occurrence of ≥ 2 asthma-coded encounters (ICD-10 J45.x / ICD-9 493.x) within any rolling 12-month window, among children with no prior asthma code in the 24-month pre-entry wash-out period.

**Effect measure reported:** Adjusted hazard ratio (aHR) with 95% confidence interval and two-sided p-value, for a 5 µg/m³ increment in annual-average residential PM2.5.

---

## Main Confound and Mitigation

### Primary Confound: Socioeconomic Deprivation
Families with lower income disproportionately reside near pollution sources (environmental justice gradient), are more likely to live in housing with mold, cockroach allergen, and environmental tobacco smoke, and have reduced healthcare access that may alter diagnosis timing. Socioeconomic deprivation therefore confounds the PM2.5–asthma relationship in both directions (positive confounding via co-occurring indoor exposures; potential negative confounding via under-diagnosis in low-access areas).

**Mitigation strategy:**
- **Direct adjustment:** Household income quintile and parental smoking status are mandatory covariates in the primary model (satisfying the hard constraint).
- **Area-level proxy:** Census-tract median income and Area Deprivation Index (ADI, publicly available from University of Wisconsin) are added as additional continuous covariates to capture neighborhood-level deprivation not captured by individual-level claims data.
- **Residual confounding acknowledgment:** The E-value sensitivity analysis (Sensitivity Analysis 4) quantifies robustness; the Discussion will explicitly state that unmeasured indoor allergen burden cannot be fully controlled without direct measurement (and note that indoor monitoring is out of scope per study constraints).
- **Note on banned move:** Indoor air-quality monitoring is explicitly excluded per the study constraints. Parental smoking status (from ICD codes) serves as the available proxy for the indoor tobacco smoke component of indoor air quality.

---

## Two Plausible Alternatives Not Chosen

### Alternative 1: Distributed-Lag Nonlinear Model (DLNM)
**Description:** A DLNM would model the simultaneous nonlinear and lagged relationship between annual PM2.5 at each prior age and asthma onset, generating a three-dimensional exposure–lag–response surface.

**Why not chosen:**
- DLNMs require a complete, evenly spaced exposure time series for every subject. In a retrospective cohort drawn from administrative data, children enter at different ages and have variable follow-up, creating irregular observation windows that substantially complicate DLNM implementation without strong parametric assumptions.
- The Cox model with a time-varying exposure and a pre-specified lag sensitivity analysis (Sensitivity Analysis 1) captures the core scientific question—does cumulative exposure increase hazard?—with lower model complexity and more transparent interpretation for a regulatory or clinical audience.
- DLNMs are better suited to time-series ecological designs; the individual-level cohort structure is better exploited by survival analysis.

### Alternative 2: Propensity-Score Matched Cohort (High vs. Low PM2.5 ZIP Codes)
**Description:** Children in ZIP codes with annual-average PM2.5 in the top quartile would be matched 1:1 or 1:2 to children in the bottom quartile using propensity scores based on demographics and SES, then followed for asthma incidence; effect estimated as an incidence rate ratio.

**Why not chosen:**
- Dichotomizing exposure into high/low ZIP-code categories discards the continuous exposure gradient and reduces statistical power, particularly important given that modern U.S. PM2.5 levels cluster in a relatively narrow range (5–15 µg/m³ in most regions post-2010).
- ZIP-code-level assignment introduces substantial exposure misclassification compared with IDW interpolation from AQS monitors to geocoded residential addresses, moving away from the precision required by the hard constraint (annual-average PM2.5 *at residential address*).
- Propensity-score matching on ZIP code cannot adequately control for within-ZIP-code SES heterogeneity, potentially worsening residual confounding relative to individual-level covariate adjustment in the Cox model.
- The Cox model preserves the full continuous exposure distribution and is more directly interpretable as a dose–response relationship, which is the central scientific and regulatory question.

---

objective: Test whether jurisdiction-level bail reform policies that eliminate cash bail for misdemeanors reduce the Black-white gap in pretrial detention rates without increasing failure-to-appear rates.
hard_constraints: quasi-experimental design using staggered policy adoption across jurisdictions, no randomization
treatment defined as statutory or administrative elimination of cash bail for all misdemeanor charges, not partial reforms or judicial discretion changes
pretrial detention measured from official jail booking records, not survey data
racial disparity must be operationalized as the Black-white ratio of pretrial detention rates, not raw counts
must include at least 4 reform-adopting jurisdictions and at least 4 comparison jurisdictions
analysis must span at least 2 years pre-reform and 2 years post-reform
failure-to-appear rate must be measured from court administrative records within the same jurisdictions
banned_moves: add a qualitative interview component with judges or defendants
expand the outcome to include felony charges alongside misdemeanors
incorporate recidivism or re-arrest as an outcome measure
use individual-level risk assessment scores as a control variable
final_proposal:
### Final Research Proposal

#### Core Hypothesis
The statutory elimination of cash bail for misdemeanors reduces the Black-white pretrial detention ratio by removing the "financial gatekeeper" effect—a mechanism disproportionately penalizing Black defendants—without increasing Failure-to-Appear (FTA) rates beyond a pre-specified non-inferiority margin. We hypothesize that the narrowing of the racial detention gap will be significantly greater in charge categories that historically carried the highest average cash bail amounts (the "high financial barrier" intensity).

#### Minimal Viable Design
We employ a **Staggered Triple-Difference (DDD) event-study design**.
1.  **Sample:** 4+ treatment jurisdictions (statutory/administrative elimination of misdemeanor cash bail) vs. 4+ control jurisdictions (status quo), matched via propensity scores on population, racial composition, and baseline detention rates.
2.  **Estimation:** Use the **Callaway-Sant’Anna (CS) estimator** for group-time average treatment effects, interacting the reform indicator with a "Bail Intensity Index" (defined by pre-reform mean bail amounts for specific misdemeanor types).
3.  **Data:** Official jail booking logs (detention status) and court administrative records (FTA/bench warrants) aggregated monthly for 2 years pre- and 2 years post-reform.

#### Primary Outcome
1.  **Equity:** The Black-white ratio of pretrial detention rates, reported with 95% confidence intervals to identify the magnitude of the gap reduction.
2.  **Safety:** The change in the FTA rate, evaluated against a pre-specified non-inferiority margin ($\delta = 1.5$ percentage points).

#### Main Confound and Mitigation
*   **Confound:** "Compositional Shift"—the possibility that the reform changes *who* is being arrested or *which* crimes are processed, creating a false signal of racial equity.
*   **Mitigation:** The **Triple-Difference (DDD)** framework accounts for this by comparing detention trends within specific charge-type strata (high- vs. low-intensity bail) across both reform and control jurisdictions, effectively isolating the policy effect from underlying changes in arrest composition.

#### Two Plausible Alternatives Not Chosen
1.  **Synthetic Control Method (SCM):** While excellent for single-unit case studies, it lacks the statistical power of the multi-jurisdiction DDD approach and is less robust to the staggered policy adoption timelines required by the brief.
2.  **Regression Discontinuity (RD):** While highly rigorous, it restricts the study to a single jurisdiction with a clear legislative "cut-off" date, failing to meet the requirement for 4+ comparison jurisdictions and potentially limiting the generalizability of the findings.

objective: Test whether algorithmic content curation on social media platforms reduces the diversity of news sources that users engage with compared to reverse-chronological feeds.
hard_constraints: observational study only, no platform-side A/B test access
must use publicly available or researcher-accessible API data
sample must include at least 5000 unique user accounts
analysis window must not exceed 6 months
must operationalize source diversity using Shannon entropy or equivalent information-theoretic metric
no survey or self-report data
banned_moves: add sentiment analysis or toxicity scoring of consumed content
expand scope to include non-news content such as entertainment or lifestyle posts
introduce a content-level topic model to characterize article similarity
final_proposal:
**Research Proposal: Testing the Impact of Algorithmic Curation on News Source Diversity in Social Media Feeds**

---

### **Core Hypothesis**  
Users exposed to algorithmically curated feeds engage with a less diverse set of news sources—measured via Shannon entropy of source distribution—than users who use reverse-chronological feeds, after accounting for user self-selection into feed types.

---

### **Minimal Viable Design**  
We conduct a **matched observational study** using publicly accessible API data from a major social media platform (e.g., Twitter/X via its Academic Research API) during a 6-month window. We identify users who explicitly opt into a reverse-chronological feed (treatment group: chronological) and compare them to users who remain on the default algorithmic feed (control group: algorithmic).  

To address selection bias, we use **propensity score matching (PSM)** based on observable user characteristics:  
- Follower/following count  
- Account age  
- Daily posting frequency  
- Historical news-sharing behavior (proportion of news links shared over total links)  
- Geolocation and language  

We restrict the sample to users who share URLs linking to domains classified as news outlets (using a pre-validated news domain list, e.g., Media Bias/Fact Check or CrowdTangle’s news taxonomy). Only users with ≥10 news link shares during the analysis window are included to ensure sufficient engagement for diversity measurement.

The analysis compares the **Shannon entropy of news source domains** per user between matched chronological and algorithmic feed users.

---

### **Primary Outcome**  
**User-level Shannon entropy of news source domains**, defined as:  

\[
H(u) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i(u) \log p_i(u)
\]

Where:  
- \( p_i(u) \) = proportion of news links shared by user \( u \) from source \( i \)  
- Summed over all unique news sources \( i \) shared by user \( u \)  

This metric is pre-registered as the **sole primary diversity metric** prior to analysis.

---

### **Main Confound and Mitigation**  
**Main confound**: Users who opt into chronological feeds may be systematically more media-literate, privacy-conscious, or ideologically motivated—traits also associated with seeking diverse news sources. This creates selection bias.  

**Mitigation**:  
- **Propensity score matching** on observable behavioral and demographic covariates (listed above) to create balanced groups.  
- **Balance diagnostics** (standardized mean differences < 0.1) to confirm covariate alignment post-matching.  
- **Sensitivity analysis** using the E-value to assess robustness to unmeasured confounding.

---

### **Two Plausible Alternatives Not Chosen**  

1. **Natural experiment with regression discontinuity (RD) around a platform-wide feed switch**  
   *Why not chosen*: While appealing, most platforms implement feed changes gradually or without clear temporal thresholds (e.g., no sharp cutoff by user ID or sign-up date). Additionally, users can revert feed settings, undermining the exogeneity assumption. The lack of a clean discontinuity in recent platform history makes RD infeasible under the 6-month window constraint.

2. **Instrumental variable (IV) approach using device type or app version**  
   *Why not chosen*: Device type (e.g., iOS vs Android) or app version may correlate with feed type due to staggered rollouts, but these instruments are weak or violate exclusion (e.g., device type may independently affect news consumption behavior). Under the hard constraint of using only public API data, validating instrument strength and exogeneity is infeasible, risking bias amplification.

---

objective: Does augmenting training data with back-translation (English to pivot language and back) improve macro-F1 for sentiment analysis when the test domain differs from the training domain by more than 5 points of baseline F1 drop?
hard_constraints: Back-translation uses a single pivot language; no multi-pivot or paraphrase models
Source training domain and target test domain must be explicitly different (e.g., electronics reviews vs restaurant reviews)
Baseline model is a fine-tuned encoder of 110-130M parameters (e.g., BERT-base or equivalent)
Augmented data must not exceed 2x the original training set size
Domain shift confirmed by at least a 5-point macro-F1 drop when the baseline trained on source is evaluated on target
Minimum 3 domain-shift pairs evaluated
banned_moves: Using generative LLM paraphrasing (e.g., GPT-based rewriting) instead of back-translation
Including any target-domain labeled data in training
Applying domain-adaptive pre-training (DAPT) on unlabeled target-domain text
Filtering or curating back-translated samples by quality score
final_proposal:
## Core hypothesis
For cross-domain sentiment analysis, when a source-trained **110–130M encoder baseline** suffers a **>5-point macro-F1 drop** on a distinct target domain, **single-pivot back-translation** of source training examples (**English→German→English**) improves target-domain **macro-F1** because it exposes the model to **label-preserving surface variation** during training. The expected gain is due to this variation, not merely to increasing training set size.

## Minimal viable design
### Data and domains
- Use a **multi-domain sentiment dataset** with explicit domain labels and consistent sentiment labels across domains.
- Evaluate **3 explicit source→target domain pairs** at minimum.
- Recommended setting: **3 Amazon review domain pairs** for consistency and feasibility.
- Retain only pairs where the baseline trained on source and evaluated on target shows a **macro-F1 drop greater than 5 points**, satisfying the domain-shift requirement.

### Model
- **BERT-base** or equivalent encoder in the **110–130M parameter** range.

### Conditions
Train and evaluate **three conditions** for each domain pair:

1. **Baseline**
   - Train on original labeled source-domain training data only.

2. **Duplication control**
   - Train on original source data plus a duplicated copy of the same source examples.
   - Total training size = **2.0x** original.

3. **Back-translation augmentation**
   - Train on original source data plus **one back-translated version** of each source example.
   - Back-translation pipeline: **English → German → English**
   - Total training size = **2.0x** original.

### Training protocol
- Use the **same architecture, tokenizer, hyperparameters, and training budget** across all conditions within each domain pair.
- Run **3 random fine-tuning seeds per condition**.

### Mechanism probe
To support the causal story:
- Take a held-out **source-domain evaluation split**
- Create a **back-translated version** of that held-out split using the same English→German→English pipeline
- Evaluate each trained model on:
  - original held-out source texts
  - back-translated held-out source texts
- Compare the **performance drop** from original to transformed text

Interpretation:
- If BT training works via increased invariance to surface reformulation, the BT-trained model should show a **smaller drop** than both baseline and duplication control.

### Secondary scope-condition analysis
For each source→target pair, compute one simple source–target lexical divergence measure, preferably:
- **unigram Jensen-Shannon divergence**

Then assess whether larger divergence is associated with larger BT gains. This is supportive, not primary.

## Primary outcome
- **Macro-F1 on the target-domain test set**

Primary comparisons:
- **BT vs Baseline**
- **BT vs Duplication control**

Statistical test:
- **paired bootstrap**, as required

Success is judged against the original brief:
- statistically significant macro-F1 improvement from augmentation on **at least 2 of 3 domain pairs**
- **average gain ≥ 1.5 macro-F1 points**
- findings hold across **3 seeds**

The **BT vs duplication** comparison is the key added evidence for the causal interpretation, though it does not replace the original success criteria.

## Main confound and mitigation
### Main confound
A reviewer may argue that any improvement from back-translation is caused simply by **doubling the amount of training data / optimization exposure**, rather than by the **surface variation introduced by back-translation**.

### Mitigation
Include the **size-matched duplication control**:
- same final training set size as BT
- same training budget
- no new linguistic variation

Interpretation:
- If BT outperforms duplication, then the gain is less plausibly explained by data volume alone.
- The source-side invariance probe further strengthens this by showing that BT specifically reduces sensitivity to back-translation-style reformulations.

## Two plausible alternatives not chosen
### 1. Compare two pivot languages on the same domain pairs
Example: **German vs Chinese**

Why plausible:
- could test whether pivot typology matters

Why not chosen:
- adds a second experimental factor not required to identify the main claim
- increases compute and interpretation burden
- weakens focus on the core causal question of whether single-pivot BT helps at all

### 2. Vary augmentation ratio as a secondary factor
Example: **0.5x vs 1.0x vs 2.0x**

Why plausible:
- could reveal dose-response behavior

Why not chosen:
- adds complexity and more runs without being necessary for the main objective
- makes the study look like an augmentation-tuning paper rather than a clean test of the target claim
- risks underpowering the core comparison across the required domain pairs and seeds

objective: Does few-shot in-context learning (5-shot) with a frozen 7B-parameter LLM achieve higher macro-F1 than task-specific fine-tuning of a 300M-parameter encoder model on text classification for languages with fewer than 10,000 labeled training examples?
hard_constraints: Few-shot model must be a single 7B-parameter decoder-only LLM with no gradient updates
Fine-tuned model must be a pre-trained multilingual encoder (e.g., XLM-R base, 270-300M parameters)
Evaluation languages must have fewer than 10,000 labeled training examples in the chosen dataset
Minimum 3 low-resource languages evaluated, each from a different language family
Primary metric is macro-F1 on a held-out test set of at least 500 examples per language
Few-shot examples selected randomly with a fixed seed; no manual curation or chain-of-thought
banned_moves: Using chain-of-thought, scratchpad, or multi-step reasoning in the few-shot prompt
Fine-tuning the 7B LLM (LoRA, adapters, or full) instead of keeping it frozen
Evaluating only on high-resource languages (more than 10,000 labeled examples)
Ensembling multiple models or combining few-shot and fine-tuned predictions
Translating test data into English before classification
final_proposal:
# Final Research Proposal

---

## Core Hypothesis

The performance advantage of fine-tuned multilingual encoders over frozen large language models in low-resource text classification is driven primarily by the LLM's output-side label association weakness: when a frozen LLM has had insufficient pretraining exposure to text in language L paired with English classification labels, it cannot reliably map native-language input representations to the correct label tokens at decoding time. This weakness is measurable before the main experiment via a Label Association Score (LAS), is predictive of the per-language performance gap, and is partially recoverable by replacing English label tokens with native-language label tokens in the prompt — without any gradient updates.

**The single falsifiable causal claim:**
> Across low-resource languages, the macro-F1 gap between fine-tuned XLM-R Base and frozen Mistral-7B (5-shot, English labels) is negatively correlated with LAS — and providing native-language labels in the prompt reduces that gap more for low-LAS languages than for high-LAS languages.

**What would falsify it:**
- LAS does not predict the gap (Spearman ρ ≈ 0 in A2)
- Native labels do not differentially help low-LAS languages (Δmacro-F1(L2 − L1) is uncorrelated with LAS in A3)
- Frozen XLM-R kNN consistently outperforms Frozen Mistral-7B regardless of LAS (A5), indicating input encoding — not label association — is the binding constraint

All three falsification conditions are tested within the design. A clean null on all three would redirect the field toward input-side accounts and is itself a publishable finding.

---

## Minimal Viable Design

### Benchmark
**SIB-200** (Adelani et al., 2023). Seven-class topic classification (science/technology, travel, politics, sports, health, entertainment, geography). Single label schema across all languages. Official train/dev/test splits used throughout; no custom splits.

### Languages
Six languages selected to span a range of estimated LAS values while satisfying all hard constraints. Each is from a distinct language family; all have fewer than 10,000 labeled training examples in SIB-200 (empirically ≤ 701).

| Language | ISO | Family | Script | Est. LAS rank | Train N |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tagalog | tgl | Austronesian | Latin | High | ~701 |
| Swahili | swa | Niger-Congo (Bantu) | Latin | Moderate-high | ~701 |
| Yoruba | yor | Niger-Congo (Volta-Niger) | Latin | Moderate-low | ~701 |
| Bengali | ben | Indo-European | Bengali | Moderate-low | ~701 |
| Burmese | mya | Sino-Tibetan | Burmese | Low | ~701 |
| Tigrinya | tir | Afro-Asiatic | Ethiopic | Very low | ~701 |

*Substitution rule: If any language's test set falls below 500 examples, substitute the closest typological neighbour with a confirmed ≥ 502 test set (Amharic for Tigrinya; Cebuano for Tagalog). Substitution decided before any model is run.*

*LAS rank is a prior expectation, pre-registered before experiments begin. Language selection is not adjusted post-hoc based on observed LAS values.*

### Models

**Model 1 — Frozen LLM:** Mistral-7B-v0.3, decoder-only, 7.24B parameters. Used for all inference conditions. No gradient updates under any condition. Inference in bf16; if 4-bit quantization is required by compute constraints, this is documented and held constant across all languages and conditions.

**Model 2 — Fine-tuned encoder:** XLM-RoBERTa Base, 270M parameters. Standard sequence classification head. Full encoder fine-tuning; cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters fixed across all languages: lr = 2e-5, batch size = 16, max epochs = 10, early stopping on dev macro-F1 with patience = 3, max sequence length = 128 tokens.

**Model 3 — Frozen encoder kNN:** XLM-RoBERTa Base, identical weights to Model 2 before fine-tuning, no gradient updates. Classification by cosine similarity between [CLS] embeddings of test input and 5 few-shot examples. Uses identical few-shot examples to Model 1 (same seeds). This is not an additional model download — it is the same checkpoint as Model 2, used before fine-tuning.

### Experimental Conditions

| ID | Model | Prompt / regime | Labels | N train | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LAS** | Mistral-7B (frozen) | 0-shot, 50-example probe | English | — | Pre-experiment predictor |
| **L1** | Mistral-7B (frozen) | 5-shot | English | — | Primary comparison |
| **L2** | Mistral-7B (frozen) | 5-shot | Native language | — | Causal intervention |
| **L3** | Mistral-7B (frozen) | 0-shot | English | — | Sub-mechanism baseline |
| **K1** | XLM-R Base (frozen) | kNN, 5 examples | Label-free | — | Falsification check |
| **F1** | XLM-R Base (fine-tuned) | Classifier | — | 701 | Primary comparison |
| **F2** | XLM-R Base (fine-tuned) | Classifier | — | 300 | Data-size sensitivity |
| **F3** | XLM-R Base (fine-tuned) | Classifier | — | 100 | Data-size sensitivity |

### Prompt Structure (L1, fixed across all languages)

```
Classify the following text into exactly one category:
science/technology, travel, politics, sports, health,
entertainment, geography.
Reply with only the category label. Do not explain.

Text: [example 1 in language L] → [correct English label]
Text: [example 2 in language L] → [correct English label]
Text: [example 3 in language L] → [correct English label]
Text: [example 4 in language L] → [correct English label]
Text: [example 5 in language L] → [correct English label]

Text: [test input in language L] →
```

L2 replaces the 7 English category names with verified native-language translations throughout the prompt (both the label list and the example labels). Test input text is never modified. L3 removes the five examples entirely, keeping the label list and test input.

*Label translations for L2: sourced from SIB-200 metadata where available; otherwise verified by cross-referencing two independent sources (e.g., a native-speaker consultant and a bilingual dictionary). The 42 translations (7 labels × 6 languages) are released in full in the paper appendix.*

### Seeds and Randomisation
Three seeds for all conditions: **42, 123, 456**.
- Few-shot example selection (L1, L2, L3, K1): pure random sampling from the training split, stratified to avoid all-same-class draws only if a pure random draw produces all 5 examples from one class (astronomically unlikely but handled explicitly).
- Fine-tuning initialisation (F1, F2, F3): controls weight initialisation of the classification head.
- LAS probe: single seed (42); not used in any significance test.
- Subsampling for F2 (N=300) and F3 (N=100): random subsets of the F1 training data, same 3 seeds, stratified by class to ensure all 7 classes are represented.

### Cell Count and Compute

| Condition | Languages | Seeds | Cells | Time/cell | GPU-hours |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| LAS probe | 6 | 1 | 6 | 5 min | 0.5 |
| L1, L2, L3 | 6 | 3 | 54 | 25 min | 22.5 |
| K1 | 6 | 3 | 18 | 5 min | 1.5 |
| F1, F2, F3 | 6 | 3 | 54 | 8 min | 7.2 |
| **Total** | | | **132** | | **~32 GPU-hours** |

Within a single GPU-week on one A100 80GB. No distributed training required.

---

## Primary Outcome

**The primary outcome is the per-language macro-F1 gap between L1 and F1, tested with a paired bootstrap procedure and reported with confidence intervals.**

### Significance test
For each of the 6 languages independently:
- Pool predictions from 3 seeds for each model (L1 and F1)
- Paired bootstrap test: resample the test set with replacement, 10,000 iterations, compute Δmacro-F1 = macro-F1(L1) − macro-F1(F1) in each resample
- Report two-tailed p-value and 95% bootstrap CI for Δmacro-F1
- Significance threshold: p < 0.05

### Pre-registered aggregate decision rule
"Few-shot wins overall" requires p < 0.05 with CI excluding zero in the positive direction for ≥ 4 of 6 languages. This threshold is set before any model is run.

### Secondary outcomes (reported but not the basis for the aggregate claim)

**A2 — LAS as predictor of the gap:**
Spearman ρ between LAS(L) and Δmacro-F1(F1 − L1) across 6 languages. Predicted sign: positive (higher LAS → smaller fine-tuning advantage). Reported with n=6 power caveat; framed as hypothesis-generating.

**A3 — Label association intervention:**
Δmacro-F1(L2 − L1) per language. Spearman ρ between LAS(L) and Δmacro-F1(L2 − L1). Predicted sign: negative (lower LAS → larger benefit from native labels).

**A4 — Example vs. label format:**
Δmacro-F1(L1 − L3) per language. Tests whether few-shot examples add value beyond zero-shot, and whether this value is larger for low-LAS languages.

**A5 — Falsification check:**
Δmacro-F1(K1 − L1) per language. If K1 > L1 on low-LAS languages, the input-encoding account gains support and the causal chain requires revision. Reported as a directional test with explicit falsification framing regardless of outcome.

**A6 — Data-size sensitivity:**
Δmacro-F1(L1 − F) plotted vs. N ∈ {100, 300, 701} per language. N-crossover point correlated with LAS(L). Predicted sign: positive (higher LAS → lower crossover N, meaning fine-tuning needs less data to compete when the LLM already has strong label associations).

### Reporting standards
- All 132 cells reported in a supplementary table (raw macro-F1, mean and std across seeds)
- Null-output rate for Mistral-7B reported per language per condition (% of test inputs producing no valid label token)
- Per-class F1 and confusion matrices in appendix
- All code, prompts, seeds, and fine-tuned checkpoints released on GitHub and HuggingFace Hub before submission

---

## Main Confound and Mitigation

### The confound
**The two models differ on three dimensions simultaneously: parameter count (7B vs. 270M), training objective (causal LM vs. masked LM), and inference regime (in-context learning vs. gradient updates).** Any observed performance gap is attributable to any combination of these three factors. The causal story assigns the gap to inference-regime-mediated label association weakness, but a sceptical reviewer can always attribute it to raw parameter count or training objective differences instead.

This is the confound that motivated the addition of the frozen XLM-R kNN baseline in the previous design iteration, and it remains the central threat to the causal interpretation.

### The mitigation
The frozen XLM-R kNN baseline (K1) breaks the three-way confound into separable contrasts:

| Contrast | Parameters | Training objective | Inference regime | What it isolates |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| K1 vs. F1 | Same (270M) | Same (masked LM) | **Different** (frozen vs. fine-tuned) | Value of gradient updates alone |
| K1 vs. L1 | **Different** (270M vs. 7B) | **Different** (masked vs. causal) | Same (both frozen, few-shot) | Value of scale + causal pretraining, holding regime constant |
| L1 vs. F1 | **Different** | **Different** | **Different** | The brief's primary comparison; all three factors vary |

The causal story predicts a specific pattern across these three contrasts:
- K1 vs. F1 gap should be **large** (gradient updates are valuable regardless of language)
- K1 vs. L1 gap should be **negatively correlated with LAS** (the LLM's scale advantage materialises only when it has strong label associations; for low-LAS languages, the kNN's label-free approach is relatively competitive)
- L1 vs. F1 gap should be **the largest for low-LAS languages** and should **shrink under L2** (native labels)

If all three patterns hold simultaneously, the parameter-count and training-objective accounts are insufficient — they predict the K1 vs. L1 gap should be uniformly large (in favour of the 7B model) regardless of LAS, which is the opposite of the predicted pattern.

### Residual limitation (stated explicitly in the paper)
The design cannot fully decorrelate parameter count from training objective, because no 7B masked LM and no 270M causal LM are included. This is acknowledged as a limitation; addressing it fully would require a dedicated scaling study outside the scope of this design. The kNN contrast is the best available mitigation within the constraints of the brief.

---

## Two Plausible Alternatives Not Chosen

### Alternative 1: Three-language minimal design with kNN anchor only

**What it is:** Return to the minimum required by the brief — 3 languages, 3 families — and include only the primary binary comparison (L1 vs. F1) plus the frozen kNN anchor (K1). Drop the LAS probe, the native-label intervention (L2), the zero-shot condition (L3), and the data-size curve. The paper's contribution is the partial factorial attribution (K1 breaks the parameter-count confound) rather than the causal LAS story.

**Why it was not chosen:** The kNN anchor alone addresses the confound but does not provide a mechanism. The paper would show *that* the gap exists and *that* it is not fully explained by parameter count, but would have nothing to say about *why* it varies across languages. Without the LAS predictor and the L2 intervention, the cross-language variation in the gap is unexplained, and reviewers will ask for it. The LAS + L2 components add approximately 8 GPU-hours and 42 label translations — a low cost for a substantially stronger paper.

**When this alternative would be preferable:** If the primary venue is a short paper track (4 pages), the findings track of a workshop, or if compute is constrained to less than 10 GPU-hours. It is also the fallback design if the native-label translations for any language cannot be verified before the submission deadline.

### Alternative 2: Cross-benchmark design with two tasks

**What it is:** Replace the single SIB-200 benchmark with two benchmarks covering different classification tasks — for example, SIB-200 (topic classification) and MasakhaNEWS (news category classification) — evaluated on overlapping languages where both benchmarks have data. The paper tests whether the LAS-gap relationship generalises across task types, not just across languages.

**Why it was not chosen:** Two benchmarks introduce label-schema heterogeneity (different category sets, different annotation guidelines, different domain distributions) that conflates task effects with language effects. The LAS probe is computed on the actual task labels, so a different label set for the second benchmark requires a separate LAS computation with a different label vocabulary — making the LAS scores non-comparable across benchmarks. More fundamentally, the brief's objective is a single comparison question; demonstrating cross-task generality is a follow-up contribution, not a requirement for answering the primary question. Adding a second benchmark increases the risk of a reviewer asking why the two tasks give different results, which the design cannot answer without a third benchmark for triangulation.

**When this alternative would be preferable:** If the primary concern is external validity of the LAS mechanism across task types, and if both benchmarks use identical or mappable label

objective: Determine whether long-term ambient PM2.5 exposure is associated with increased childhood asthma incidence after adjusting for socioeconomic and indoor-environment confounders.
hard_constraints: retrospective cohort design only, no randomization of exposure
exposure metric must be annual-average PM2.5 at residential address, not self-reported
cohort must include at least 5000 children aged 0-12
must use ICD-coded asthma diagnoses, not survey-reported symptoms
must adjust for at least household income and parental smoking status
no proprietary air-quality datasets; only publicly available EPA AQS or equivalent
banned_moves: add an indoor air-quality monitoring component to the study
expand the outcome to include all respiratory conditions rather than asthma specifically
incorporate genetic or biomarker data to explore biologic mechanisms
final_proposal:
**Final Proposal**

**Core Hypothesis:**
Long-term exposure to ambient PM2.5 increases the risk of childhood asthma incidence by inducing chronic inflammation and oxidative stress in the lungs.

**Minimal Viable Design:**
We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using a distributed-lag nonlinear model (DLNM) to examine the relationship between annual-average PM2.5 exposure and childhood asthma incidence. The study will include at least 5000 children aged 0-12, with PM2.5 exposure estimated using satellite-derived data and land-use regression. We will adjust for socioeconomic and environmental confounders, including household income, parental education, and access to green spaces.

**Primary Outcome:**
The primary outcome will be ICD-coded asthma diagnoses, which will be used to define the incidence of childhood asthma.

**Main Confound and Mitigation:**
The main confound of interest is socioeconomic status, which can affect both PM2.5 exposure and asthma incidence. To mitigate this confound, we will adjust for household income, parental education, and other socioeconomic factors in our regression model. We will also use a spatial regression model to account for spatial autocorrelation and confounding.

**Two Plausible Alternatives Not Chosen:**

1. **Case-control design:** While a case-control design could be used to examine the association between PM2.5 exposure and childhood asthma, it would be subject to recall bias and may not accurately capture the temporal relationship between exposure and outcome.
2. **Panel study:** A panel study could provide valuable insights into the relationship between PM2.5 exposure and childhood asthma, but it would require significant resources and may not be feasible with the current budget and timeline.

objective: Test whether active removal of a dominant invasive plant species increases native plant percent cover within 3 years at restoration sites compared to untreated control sites.
hard_constraints: must target a single named invasive species, not multiple invaders simultaneously
paired design with at least 8 treatment-control site pairs
native cover must be measured as percent ground cover in fixed quadrats, not species counts
all sites must be within the same ecoregion
removal method must be mechanical only, no herbicide application
baseline data must be collected before removal begins
banned_moves: add a seeding or active replanting treatment to accelerate native recovery
expand monitoring to include soil microbiome or nutrient cycling outcomes
include a herbicide-treatment arm for comparison with mechanical removal
final_proposal:
**Final Proposal**

**Core Hypothesis**:
Mechanically removing a single dominant invasive plant species from restoration sites within the same ecoregion will increase native plant percent cover compared to untreated control sites over a 3-year period.

**Minimal Viable Design**:
A before-after-control-impact (BACI) design with a paired treatment-control site approach, where at least 8 pairs of sites are selected, and the invasive species is mechanically removed from the treatment sites. Native plant percent cover will be measured in fixed quadrats at baseline and annually for 3 years.

**Primary Outcome**:
The primary outcome is the difference in native plant percent cover between treatment and control sites over the 3-year period, estimated using a linear mixed model (LMM) with site pair as a random effect and year as a repeated measure.

**Main Confound and Mitigation**:
The main confound is the potential for differences in baseline native cover between treatment and control sites, which could influence the outcome. To mitigate this, the study will ensure that treatment and control sites are paired based on similarity in invasive species density and native plant cover, and that the difference in baseline native cover between treatment and control sites is not more than 10 percentage points.

**Two Plausible Alternatives Not Chosen**:
1. **Bayesian Change-Point Model**: This approach could estimate the lag between removal and native cover recovery, but it was not chosen because it may require more complex modeling and interpretation, and may not directly address the core hypothesis.
2. **Permutation-Based Test**: This approach could test the treatment effect using site-level slopes of native cover over time, but it was not chosen because it may not account for the paired design and repeated measures, and may not provide a direct estimate of the treatment effect.

objective: Test whether algorithmic content curation on social media platforms reduces the diversity of news sources that users engage with compared to reverse-chronological feeds.
hard_constraints: observational study only, no platform-side A/B test access
must use publicly available or researcher-accessible API data
sample must include at least 5000 unique user accounts
analysis window must not exceed 6 months
must operationalize source diversity using Shannon entropy or equivalent information-theoretic metric
no survey or self-report data
banned_moves: add sentiment analysis or toxicity scoring of consumed content
expand scope to include non-news content such as entertainment or lifestyle posts
introduce a content-level topic model to characterize article similarity
final_proposal:
**Final Proposal:**

**Core Hypothesis:**
Algorithmic content curation on social media platforms reduces the diversity of news sources that users engage with compared to reverse-chronological feeds.

**Minimal Viable Design:**
A quasi-experimental design leveraging a natural experiment: a platform-wide default feed switch from reverse-chronological to algorithmic curation. We will collect data on user engagement with news sources over a period of 3 months before and 3 months after the switch, using a regression discontinuity design (RDD) to compare the source diversity of users who were exposed to the algorithmic feed (post-switch) to those who were exposed to the reverse-chronological feed (pre-switch).

**Primary Outcome:**
The primary outcome is the change in source diversity, operationalized using Shannon entropy, between the pre-switch and post-switch periods.

**Main Confound and Mitigation:**
The main confound is user self-selection into algorithmically curated vs chronological feeds. To mitigate this, we will use a regression discontinuity design (RDD) to compare the source diversity of users who were exposed to the algorithmic feed (post-switch) to those who were exposed to the reverse-chronological feed (pre-switch). Additionally, we will control for user demographics, engagement patterns, and device type using machine learning techniques (e.g., propensity score matching).

**Two Plausible Alternatives Not Chosen:**

1. **Matched-pair comparison of users who opt into chronological feeds vs those who remain on algorithmic feeds using propensity score matching**: While this design could account for user self-selection, it would not provide a clear causal inference, as users who opt into chronological feeds may differ from those who remain on algorithmic feeds in ways that affect source diversity.
2. **Instrumental variable approach using device type or app version as an instrument for feed algorithm exposure**: While this design could provide a clear causal inference, it would require strong assumptions about the validity of the instrument, and may not be feasible with the available data.

objective: Test whether providing written formative feedback three times per week instead of once per week improves end-of-term exam scores in an introductory university statistics course.
hard_constraints: quasi-experimental design comparing two sections of the same course taught by the same instructor
minimum 50 students per section
feedback must be written comments on weekly problem sets, not grades or scores alone
final exam must be identical across both sections and graded by a blinded marker
study duration is exactly one academic term (10 to 15 weeks)
no additional tutoring or supplementary instruction offered to either group
banned_moves: add a third section receiving peer feedback instead of instructor feedback
measure student satisfaction or perceived learning as a co-primary outcome
vary the content or difficulty of the problem sets between sections
use automated AI-generated feedback instead of instructor-written feedback
final_proposal:
### Core Hypothesis
Providing written formative feedback three times per week instead of once per week allows students to correct misconceptions mid-week before they compound, thereby significantly improving end-of-term final exam scores. This effect is hypothesized to be moderated by feedback uptake (i.e., the intervention only works for students who consistently access the feedback within 48 hours).

### Minimal Viable Design
*   **Participants & Structure:** Two concurrent sections of an introductory statistics course taught by the same instructor over exactly one academic term (10 to 15 weeks). Each section contains a minimum of 50 students. Sections are randomly assigned to Treatment (3x/week feedback) or Control (1x/week feedback). 
*   **Pre-test:** In Week 1, all students complete an ungraded math/statistics pre-test to establish baseline ability.
*   **Identical Behavioral Protocol:** To prevent study-habit confounds, *both* sections are required to submit 1/3 of the identical weekly problem set on Monday, Wednesday, and Friday. No additional tutoring or supplementary instruction is offered to either group.
*   **The Intervention (Feedback Timing):**
    *   *Treatment:* The instructor provides written formative comments within 24 hours of each submission (delivered Mon, Wed, Fri).
    *   *Control:* The instructor withholds early comments and provides one consolidated document of written formative comments over the weekend covering all three submissions.
    *   *Strict Constraint:* Feedback in both conditions consists exclusively of written comments. No standalone grades or scores are provided on these problem sets.
*   **Uptake Measurement:** The university's Learning Management System (LMS) tracks the proportion of students in each section who access/open their written feedback documents within 48 hours of posting.

### Primary Outcome
The primary outcome is the **final exam score**. The exam will be identical across both sections, anonymised (section identifiers removed), and graded by an independent marker blinded to the experimental conditions. 

Data will be analysed using a pre-registered linear model (ANCOVA) testing the main effect of the feedback condition on final exam scores, controlling for baseline ability (Week 1 pre-test score). Individual feedback uptake (percentage of feedback accessed within 48 hours) will be included as a moderator. The treatment effect will be reported as Cohen’s *d* alongside a 95% Confidence Interval.

### Main Confound and Mitigation
**The Confound:** *The Spacing Effect (Distributed vs. Massed Practice).* If the 3x/week feedback group submitted work three times a week while the 1x/week group submitted work once a week, reviewers would argue that score improvements were caused by the Treatment group pacing their study time, not by the feedback frequency itself. (Note: Modifying submission schedules to match feedback schedules is explicitly refused as it fatally confounds the causal mechanism).
**The Mitigation:** Strict procedural control over student behavior. By forcing *both* sections to submit work on the identical Monday/Wednesday/Friday schedule, student effort and spacing are held constant. Only the independent variable—the instructor's feedback delivery schedule—is manipulated. 

### Two Plausible Alternatives Not Chosen
1.  **Compare consecutive semesters using the prior semester as a control:** Rejected because it is highly vulnerable to historical bias. Differences in term-specific disruptions, cohorts, or campus policies could confound the results. Comparing concurrent sections holds the timeline constant.
2.  **Use propensity score matching on prior GPA to adjust for selection bias:** Rejected in favour of a domain-specific pre-test (ANCOVA). While matching on GPA is viable, administering a Week 1 statistics pre-test is statistically simpler, strictly measures relevant baseline ability, requires fewer assumptions, and achieves *minimal sufficient complexity* without needing to pull separate university registrar datasets.

***

objective: Design an experiment to test whether a specific transcription factor regulates expression of a target gene under hypoxic conditions.
hard_constraints: cell culture only, no animal models
budget under 15000 GBP
must use at least one loss-of-function approach
no CRISPR screens, only targeted knockdown or knockout
must include appropriate normalization controls
banned_moves: add RNA-seq or other genome-wide profiling
introduce a second transcription factor without justification
expand scope to in vivo validation
final_proposal:
## Core hypothesis
Under hypoxic conditions, the specified transcription factor positively or negatively regulates expression of the target gene in cultured cells. If this regulation is direct, then reducing transcription factor function will alter target gene expression under hypoxia, and the transcription factor will show hypoxia-dependent occupancy and/or promoter dependence at the target gene regulatory region.

## Minimal viable design
### Overview
Use a **targeted loss-of-function approach by siRNA knockdown** of the transcription factor in a relevant cultured cell line, compare **normoxia vs hypoxia**, and measure the **target gene mRNA by RT-qPCR** as the primary endpoint. To distinguish **direct from indirect regulation**, add a **minimal promoter-reporter validation step** using wild-type versus transcription factor-binding-site-mutant promoter constructs.

This is the simplest design that satisfies:
- cell culture only
- targeted loss-of-function
- normalization controls
- positive hypoxia control
- direct vs indirect discrimination
- primary endpoint by qPCR

### Experimental setup
#### Cell system
- One cell line that:
  - expresses the transcription factor
  - induces a robust hypoxic response
  - expresses the target gene at measurable baseline levels

Use a single cell line unless there is a strong biological reason otherwise, to keep cost and complexity low.

#### Conditions
Factorial design:
1. **Control siRNA + normoxia**
2. **Control siRNA + hypoxia**
3. **TF siRNA + normoxia**
4. **TF siRNA + hypoxia**

Suggested hypoxia exposure:
- 1% O2 for 16–24 h  
This is typically sufficient for hypoxia-responsive transcriptional effects while remaining practical.

#### Loss-of-function
- Transfect **targeted siRNA** against the transcription factor
- Include **non-targeting siRNA** control
- Verify knockdown by:
  - RT-qPCR for TF mRNA and/or
  - Western blot for TF protein if antibody quality is reliable

siRNA is preferred here over CRISPRi because it is cheaper, faster, and sufficient for a focused hypothesis test under the budget cap.

#### Positive control for hypoxic stimulus
Include at least one established hypoxia-responsive gene/protein as a **stimulus control**, for example:
- **CA9**, **VEGFA**, or **GLUT1/SLC2A1** by qPCR
and/or
- **HIF-1α** stabilization by Western blot

This confirms that the hypoxic treatment worked.

#### Primary readout
- Measure **target gene mRNA by RT-qPCR**
- Normalize to **validated housekeeping genes** that are stable across normoxia and hypoxia, ideally two reference genes such as:
  - **RPLP0**
  - **TBP**
  - **HPRT1**
Avoid relying on GAPDH alone because hypoxia can alter glycolytic genes.

#### Direct-vs-indirect discrimination
Add a focused mechanistic assay:
- **Luciferase reporter assay** with:
  - wild-type target gene promoter/enhancer fragment containing the predicted TF-binding site
  - matched mutant construct with the site disrupted

Test reporter activity in:
- control siRNA vs TF siRNA
- normoxia vs hypoxia

Interpretation:
- If hypoxia induces reporter activity in the wild-type construct but not the mutant, and this induction is reduced by TF knockdown, that supports **direct promoter-dependent regulation**.
- If endogenous target gene changes with TF knockdown but reporter is unaffected, that suggests **indirect regulation** or regulation outside the cloned element.

This is a practical way to address directness without adding high-cost or genome-wide methods.

### Replication
- **3 independent biological replicates**
- Technical qPCR triplicates within each biological replicate

### Statistical plan
For the primary endpoint:
- Two-way ANOVA with factors:
  - oxygen condition
  - siRNA condition
- Key test: interaction term, indicating whether TF loss alters the hypoxic response of the target gene

### Budget fit
This design should be feasible under **£15,000**, roughly covering:
- cell culture reagents
- siRNAs and transfection reagents
- hypoxia chamber access or incubator time
- RNA extraction and cDNA/qPCR reagents
- reporter plasmid synthesis/mutagenesis
- luciferase assay reagents
- limited Western blot validation

It avoids expensive knockout generation, screening, or omics.

## Primary outcome
**Primary endpoint:** change in **target gene mRNA abundance by RT-qPCR** in **TF siRNA + hypoxia** relative to **control siRNA + hypoxia**, normalized to stable housekeeping genes.

This is the single primary endpoint and directly matches the success criterion requiring qPCR or Western blot.

## Main confound and mitigation
### Main confound
**Hypoxia and siRNA treatment may independently affect cell state or reference gene stability**, producing apparent changes in target gene expression unrelated to specific TF regulation.

### Mitigation
- Use **non-targeting siRNA** control
- Validate **TF knockdown efficiency**
- Use **two stable housekeeping genes** validated under normoxia and hypoxia
- Include a **positive hypoxia-response control gene/protein**
- Monitor viability/morphology to exclude severe toxicity
- Keep hypoxia duration consistent across all groups

## Two plausible alternatives not chosen
### 1. CRISPRi knockdown with qPCR readout under normoxia vs hypoxia
**Why plausible:** satisfies targeted loss-of-function and could provide more sustained repression than siRNA.  
**Why not chosen:** more setup time and reagent complexity than siRNA, often requiring stable dCas9-KRAB expression or additional optimization. For a minimal viable, budget-conscious design, siRNA is simpler.

### 2. siRNA knockdown with Western blot validation of target gene expression
**Why plausible:** directly measures protein-level regulation and may be more biologically relevant if the target gene is mainly regulated post-transcriptionally.  
**Why not chosen:** depends heavily on antibody quality and protein abundance, and protein turnover can blur direct transcriptional effects. qPCR is more sensitive and cleaner for a transcription-regulation question.

objective: Test whether dual-task interference on a primary visual working memory task is larger when the concurrent secondary task is visual rather than auditory, holding secondary-task difficulty constant.
hard_constraints: within-subjects design with exactly 3 conditions (single-task baseline, visual dual-task, auditory dual-task)
sample size between 40 and 80 participants
secondary tasks must be matched on standalone accuracy within 5 percentage points via pilot calibration
primary task must use a change-detection paradigm with set size 4
no neuroimaging or physiological measures
experiment must be completable in a single session under 90 minutes
banned_moves: add a cross-modal (audiovisual) secondary task condition
vary the set size of the primary task across blocks
include an individual-differences analysis correlating working memory capacity with interference magnitude
final_proposal:
## Core hypothesis
Dual-task interference on a **visual change-detection** primary task will be **larger when the concurrent secondary task is visual rather than auditory**, even when secondary-task difficulty is matched. The causal claim is that the visual secondary task competes with **shared visual selection/maintenance resources**, whereas the auditory task does not.

## Minimal viable design
- **Design:** within-subjects, exactly **3 conditions**
  1. **Single-task baseline**: visual change detection only
  2. **Visual dual-task**: change detection + visual secondary task
  3. **Auditory dual-task**: change detection + auditory secondary task
- **Primary task:** change-detection paradigm with **set size 4** in all conditions
- **Secondary tasks:**
  - **Visual:** rapid visual search / target discrimination with speeded response
  - **Auditory:** speeded auditory discrimination with the same response format
- **Calibration:** pilot and participant-level calibration to match standalone secondary-task accuracy within **5 percentage points**
- **Session:** one session, under **90 minutes**
- **Sample size:** **48–60** participants is a practical target within the allowed 40–80 range

## Primary outcome
- **Primary outcome:** **change-detection sensitivity (d-prime)**
- Compare d-prime across the three conditions
- Planned contrast: **visual-dual interference minus auditory-dual interference**
  - operationally, the difference in d-prime drop from baseline between the visual and auditory dual-task conditions

## Main confound and mitigation
### Main confound
The biggest threat is that the visual and auditory secondary tasks may differ in **difficulty**, so any larger interference from the visual task could be attributed to higher load rather than modality-specific competition.

### Mitigation
- **Pilot calibrate** the visual and auditory secondary tasks to match standalone accuracy within **5 percentage points**
- Use the **same response format**, timing structure, and feedback rules where possible
- Keep the **secondary task placement fixed** within each trial to isolate competition with the primary memory process
- Include an exclusion criterion for participants performing **below chance in any single condition**

## Two plausible alternatives not chosen
1. **Storage-demanding visual secondary task**
   - Example: brief spatial array memorization
   - Not chosen because it is less mechanistically specific than a visual selection/control task for explaining interference with visual change detection

2. **Dual-component visual secondary task**
   - Example: brief storage plus speeded processing
   - Not chosen because it is more complex and harder to calibrate cleanly within the 90-minute single-session constraint

objective: Does augmenting training data with back-translation (English to pivot language and back) improve macro-F1 for sentiment analysis when the test domain differs from the training domain by more than 5 points of baseline F1 drop?
hard_constraints: Back-translation uses a single pivot language; no multi-pivot or paraphrase models
Source training domain and target test domain must be explicitly different (e.g., electronics reviews vs restaurant reviews)
Baseline model is a fine-tuned encoder of 110-130M parameters (e.g., BERT-base or equivalent)
Augmented data must not exceed 2x the original training set size
Domain shift confirmed by at least a 5-point macro-F1 drop when the baseline trained on source is evaluated on target
Minimum 3 domain-shift pairs evaluated
banned_moves: Using generative LLM paraphrasing (e.g., GPT-based rewriting) instead of back-translation
Including any target-domain labeled data in training
Applying domain-adaptive pre-training (DAPT) on unlabeled target-domain text
Filtering or curating back-translated samples by quality score
final_proposal:
## Core hypothesis
Single-pivot back-translation improves cross-domain sentiment macro-F1 because it creates label-preserving surface variants of source-domain training examples, reducing reliance on source-specific wording. Therefore, under confirmed domain shift, back-translation should outperform both:
- a baseline trained on original source data only, and
- a size-matched duplication control that increases training quantity without adding linguistic variation.

## Minimal viable design
- **Task**: cross-domain sentiment classification
- **Model**: BERT-base or equivalent encoder with 110–130M parameters
- **Pivot language**: German only
- **Data**:
  - one multi-domain sentiment benchmark
  - at least 3 explicit source→target domain pairs
  - include only pairs where the baseline trained on source shows at least a 5-point macro-F1 drop on target relative to source-domain evaluation
- **Training conditions**:
  1. **Baseline**: original source-domain labeled training set
  2. **Duplication control**: source training examples duplicated to match the total number of instances in the BT condition
  3. **Back-translation**: original source training set plus one English→German→English version per source example
- **Augmentation cap**:
  - one BT copy per original example
  - total training size = at most 2x original
- **Runs**:
  - 3 random fine-tuning seeds per condition
  - same hyperparameters and model-selection rule across conditions
- **Evaluation**:
  - target-domain test macro-F1 is the main endpoint
  - paired bootstrap significance tests for:
    - BT vs baseline
    - BT vs duplication control
- **Validity check**:
  - human label-preservation audit on a random sample of back-translated training examples
  - diagnostic only; no filtering or removal of samples based on audit results
- **Diagnostic reporting**:
  - source-domain in-domain macro-F1 for all three conditions, to assess whether gains are transfer-specific rather than generic

## Primary outcome
Target-domain **macro-F1**.

Primary success pattern:
- back-translation yields statistically significant improvement over baseline on at least 2 of 3 domain pairs using paired bootstrap, and
- average gain over baseline is at least 1.5 macro-F1 across tested pairs, and
- results are stable across 3 seeds.

Mechanism-strengthening comparison:
- back-translation should also outperform the size-matched duplication control on target-domain macro-F1 on most tested pairs.

## Main confound and mitigation
**Main confound**:
- Any gain from back-translation could be attributed simply to having more training instances, not to the linguistic variation introduced by back-translation.

**Mitigation**:
- Add a **size-matched duplication control** with the same total number of training instances as the BT condition.
- Interpretation:
  - **BT > baseline** suggests augmentation helps.
  - **BT > duplication control** suggests the benefit is not just data quantity, but the altered wording produced by back-translation.
- Support this interpretation with a **label-preservation audit**, showing that back-translated examples usually retain the original sentiment label.

## Two plausible alternatives not chosen
1. **French as the single pivot instead of German**
   - Still compliant with the brief.
   - Not chosen because changing pivot language does not strengthen identification of the main claim as much as keeping one fixed pivot and adding a stronger control.

2. **German pivot with 4 domain pairs instead of 3**
   - Also compliant and potentially stronger statistically.
   - Not chosen for the minimal viable version because 3 pairs already satisfy the hard constraint, and keeping the study smaller preserves feasibility while testing the core claim cleanly.

objective: Test whether delayed AI responses improve human research-question generation quality.
hard_constraints: human participants only
budget under 25000 GBP
no more than 2 experimental conditions
must measure question quality with a predefined rubric
no eye tracking or neuroimaging
banned_moves: add a third experimental condition
add a think-aloud protocol or cognitive load measure
expand to longitudinal deployment beyond a single session
final_proposal:
### Final Research Proposal: The Latency-Reflection Effect

**Core Hypothesis**
Introducing an artificial latency (delay) into AI responses disrupts mindless rapid-prompting and forces cognitive reflection, thereby improving the overall scientific quality of human-generated research questions compared to immediate AI responses.

**Minimal Viable Design**
A single-session, two-arm, between-subjects randomized controlled experiment (N=200). 
*   **Task:** Participants read a brief, standardized primer on a complex topic ("Urban micro-plastics"). They are instructed to brainstorm and refine ideas using an embedded AI chat assistant before submitting three final research questions.
*   **Conditions:**
    1.  *Control (Immediate AI):* The AI responds at standard computational speed (~1–2 seconds).
    2.  *Treatment (Delayed AI):* The AI's response is artificially held for exactly 15 seconds, accompanied by a standard "Processing..." UI animation. 
*   *Note on isolation:* Both conditions utilize the exact same custom chat interface and underlying LLM. The only manipulated variable is the time-lock delay, successfully isolating the delay effect from interface novelty.

**Primary Outcome**
The mean composite quality score of the participant's three final submitted research questions. These questions will be evaluated by two blinded, independent human expert raters using a predefined 1–5 scoring rubric measuring three specific dimensions: *Originality*, *Clarity*, and *Feasibility*. 

**Main Confound and Mitigation**
*   **Confound:** Pre-existing domain expertise. Participants with high prior knowledge of micro-plastics may generate high-quality questions independently of the AI's response latency, skewing the results if unevenly distributed.
*   **Mitigation:** Stratified randomization. Before the task begins, participants complete a rapid 3-question knowledge screener. The system uses these scores to stratify assignment, ensuring an equal distribution of high- and low-knowledge participants across both the Immediate and Delayed conditions.

**Two Plausible Alternatives Not Chosen**
1.  **Alternative 1: Delayed AI vs. No-AI Control.** 
    *   *Why it was rejected:* This design conflates two independent variables: the *presence* of AI assistance and the *speed* of AI assistance. It fails the success criterion requiring the rigorous isolation of the delay effect. 
2.  **Alternative 2: Short Delay vs. Long Delay (Dose-Response).**
    *   *Why it was rejected:* Testing a dose-response (e.g., 5 seconds vs. 15 seconds) is premature for a proof-of-concept study. To achieve minimal sufficient complexity, the study must first establish whether the maximum realistic contrast (Immediate vs. Delayed) yields any measurable effect. Furthermore, including an immediate baseline alongside short and long delays would require three conditions, directly violating a hard constraint.

***

objective: Determine whether long-term ambient PM2.5 exposure is associated with increased childhood asthma incidence after adjusting for socioeconomic and indoor-environment confounders.
hard_constraints: retrospective cohort design only, no randomization of exposure
exposure metric must be annual-average PM2.5 at residential address, not self-reported
cohort must include at least 5000 children aged 0-12
must use ICD-coded asthma diagnoses, not survey-reported symptoms
must adjust for at least household income and parental smoking status
no proprietary air-quality datasets; only publicly available EPA AQS or equivalent
banned_moves: add an indoor air-quality monitoring component to the study
expand the outcome to include all respiratory conditions rather than asthma specifically
incorporate genetic or biomarker data to explore biologic mechanisms
final_proposal:
### Research Proposal: Impact of PM2.5 on Childhood Asthma Incidence

#### Core Hypothesis
Long-term exposure to higher annual-average ambient PM2.5 is positively associated with the hazard of a first-time ICD-coded asthma diagnosis in children aged 0–12, independent of socioeconomic status and household smoke exposure.

#### Minimal Viable Design
**Study Type:** Retrospective cohort study.
**Data Sources:** Linkage of state-level Medicaid/private insurance billing records (for ICD-coded asthma diagnoses) with geocoded residential addresses. PM2.5 data will be sourced from the EPA Air Quality System (AQS) via annual-average interpolation using an inverse-distance weighting (IDW) approach from monitors within a 30km radius of the residential centroid.
**Cohort:** ≥ 5,000 children, aged 0–12, residing in the study region for at least 3 consecutive years.
**Statistical Approach:** A Cox proportional hazards model with time-varying covariates. The model will estimate the Hazard Ratio (HR) for incident asthma per 5 µg/m³ increase in annual PM2.5 exposure, adjusting for age, sex, household income (neighborhood-level proxy), and parental smoking status (ICD-coded diagnosis/history in electronic health records).
**Sensitivity Analysis:** Re-running the primary model using a "2-year lagged" exposure window (exposure 2 years prior to diagnosis) to assess the robustness of the latency period.

#### Primary Outcome
Incident asthma diagnosis, defined as the first occurrence of an ICD-10 code (J45.x) following a period of at least 12 months with no asthma-related claims.

#### Main Confound and Mitigation
**Confounder:** Household-level socioeconomic status and parental smoking.
**Mitigation:** Inclusion of household income (linked via geocoded census tract data) and parental smoking status (extracted from longitudinal insurance medical history) as time-varying adjustment variables in the multivariate Cox model.

#### Two Plausible Alternatives Not Chosen
1.  **Distributed-Lag Nonlinear Model (DLNM):** While robust for capturing non-linear relationships, this requires complex data structures for satellite-derived estimates that exceed the "minimal viable" requirement and increases the risk of overfitting in a standard retrospective cohort.
2.  **Propensity Score Matching (PSM):** While excellent for balancing high-exposure/low-exposure groups, PSM significantly reduces the effective sample size and requires dropping unmatched children, potentially violating the "5,000 children" constraint if the cohort is not sufficiently large.

***
